sessions.session_title: From Vibe Coding to Vericoding: Writing Provably bug-free Code with AI
Introduction to the Math Behind Transformers and LLMs
sessions.session_abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) are everywhere. They are extremely powerful, versatile and useful. However,  many Data Scientists only use them as a tool, not getting to grips with understanding how they work from a computational and mathematical perspective, nor understanding why they give the output that they do. Yet, at their heart LLMs use simple mathematical ideas and tools, such “attention” and “transformer” blocks. Those mathematical ideas at the centre of every LLM are accessible to every Data Scientist with a grounding in the basics of linear algebra, probability, and machine learning. 

In this bootcamp session, David Hoyle (author of the Packt book ""15 Math Concepts Every Data Scientist Should Know"") will provide a high-level explanation of the core math concepts behind LLMs and Transformers, using the basic linear algebra and probability knowledge that many Data Scientists will already have. The training will focus on the “attention” mechanism and how transformers use attention to predict the next token in a sequence of tokens. The training will introduce the key concepts in the first lesson and then integrate the concepts together in the second lesson, where attendees will learn how to perform some basic but illustrative calculations in Python. The session is intended to be introductory. It will not make you a prompting expert, nor will it make you an expert in training multi-billion parameter foundational models.

sessions.session_title: Open Challenges for the Next Generation of Programming Agents
sessions.session_abstract: Programming agents have made tremendous progress recently, to the point where many organizations can rely on them to produce most of their code. Despite this success, a number of open problems remain to push these tools to the next level of productivity. In this presentation, I will outline some of these open problems and provide an overview of some ongoing research aimed at addressing these limitations.
sessions.company: MIT Schwarzman College of Computing | CSAIL

sessions.session_title: Engineering the Harness: A Practical Workshop on Context Engineering for Generative AI
sessions.session_abstract:
Many Generative AI systems struggle in production not because of model quality, but because the surrounding harness is poorly engineered. This workshop treats context engineering as a hands-on systems discipline focused on building reliable, controllable, and evaluable applications.
The workshop begins with retrieval, since every downstream decision depends on the initial context window. Participants will work through dense, lexical, and hybrid retrieval designs, learning why lexical methods remain critical for precision, constraints, and exact matching. We will also examine the tradeoffs of introducing reasoning models into agentic retrieval loops.
Next, the workshop focuses on memory as an architectural choice. We break down short-term state, long-term memory, and derived artifacts, comparing file-based, database-backed, and structured state approaches. Through concrete examples, participants will see how poor memory design leads to hallucinations, drift, and rising costs, and how selective persistence improves system stability.
Finally, the workshop explores single-agent versus multi-agent architectures. Participants will analyze supervisor and routing patterns, learning when parallelization improves throughput and when it introduces coordination overhead and new failure modes.
By the end, attendees will have a practical playbook for engineering the harness behind production-grade agentic systems.

sessions.session_title: The (AI) Data Scientist is not a software engineer
sessions.session_abstract:
Humans have developed powerful ways to represent and visualize data—tools that help us quickly extract meaning from raw data. But those same representations aren’t how AI naturally understands the world, which is why many generic AI copilots struggle with real data science tasks.

In this talk, we’ll explore what it means to treat data as its own modality—and why that shift is critical to building truly capable AI systems for analytics. 

At Sphinx AI, we’ve built an AI data scientist designed to work for data teams, not just assist them as a copilot. We’ll show how data understanding is the missing link that lets agents like ours autonomously take on messy, real-world analyses like data exploration, cleaning, modeling, and insight generation.

sessions.session_title: Compliance, Governance, and Agility — Pick 3. Optimizing Data and AI Systems in Highly Regulated Industries
sessions.session_abstract:
Data science teams need to move fast — experiment freely, access data without waiting, and iterate without fear of breaking production. Governance and compliance teams need the opposite — auditability, reproducibility, provenance, and proof that policies were followed. In most organizations, these goals are in tension. Speed often means copying data, skipping reviews, and working in untracked silos. Compliance means documentation, approval chains, and manual reconstruction of dataset lineage. The result: teams either move fast and struggle with audits, or satisfy auditors and ship slowly.

This session demonstrates a practical approach that significantly reduces the friction between these competing needs. Through a live demo with realistic data and workflows, the session shows how applying Git-like version control operations to the data layer changes the equation for both data science and compliance teams.

The session covers how to capture metadata and lineage at the point of data ingestion so it survives every downstream transformation. How branching enables safe, parallel experimentation without copying data or risking production state. How atomic operations improve recovery from pipeline failures — minutes instead of hours. How policy enforcement at the data boundary replaces manual review bottlenecks. And how immutable snapshots make compliance and auditability a byproduct of the normal workflow rather than a months-long reconstruction project.

sessions.session_title: Engineering Trust in Enterprise AI
sessions.session_abstract:
AI risk is usually treated like a compliance problem. But because AI models are probabilistic, they are guaranteed to make mistakes. As we give these systems more access to enterprise data and automated workflows, the old "check the box" approach is starting to fail. We need a new mindset and new tools to build reliable systems around these models that we can actually trust.

This session introduces Trust Engineering, a discipline that bridges the gap between the teams building models and the teams managing risk. You’ll learn how to:

Reframe AI risk as a design and engineering constraint.

Treat trust as a functional requirement rather than a compliance task.

Ask the right questions about the infrastructure needed to support a defensible system.

sessions.session_title: Deploying a Secure Autonomous Agent for Real-Time Compliance Monitoring
sessions.session_abstract:
Abstract: Autonomous AI agents are showing up everywhere right now. OpenClaw alone has over a million users running agents that can browse the web, read and write files, send emails and execute shell commands on their behalf. The problem is that most people setting these up are giving the agent the keys to the kingdom and hoping for the best. Between prompt injection attacks, over-permissioned deployments and API keys sitting in plain text, there is a lot that can go wrong when an agent has unsupervised access to your systems.

In this workshop we're going to deploy an OpenClaw agent the right way. Instead of running it on a personal machine where a compromised agent could access your files and local network, we'll deploy it on Cloudflare using their sandbox container infrastructure. The agent runs in an isolated environment with no access to anything we haven't explicitly given it. We'll set up a dedicated email for the agent, store all API keys in environment variables rather than passing them directly, scope permissions down to only what the agent needs and limit the installed skills to a vetted minimum. The goal is a deployment where even if something goes wrong with the agent, the blast radius is contained.

Once the agent is locked down, we'll put it to work on a real-world use case. The SEC's EDGAR system provides free, real-time access to regulatory filings through RSS feeds and REST APIs that update as filings are submitted. We'll configure our agent to monitor EDGAR for new compliance-relevant filings, pull the documents and deposit them into a Google Drive folder. That folder connects directly to a RAG data source so that the information is automatically available for retrieval the moment it lands. The result is a compliance knowledge base that stays current without anyone manually checking for updates.

This is a pattern that extends well beyond SEC filings. Any domain where regulations, codes or standards get updated periodically — healthcare, construction, financial services, environmental compliance — can benefit from an agent that watches for changes and keeps your data source current. We just happen to be using SEC filings because they update frequently enough to see it work live in a 2-hour workshop.

sessions.session_title: Rethinking the Operational Flow of Data Engineering
sessions.session_abstract:
For all the progress in modern data platforms, the core workflow of data engineering has changed very little. Business owners and data engineers agree on logic by screen sharing SQL, that logic gets buried in thousands of files, and everyone waits days, weeks, or months for the result to appear in a dashboard, report, or email.

What is interesting is that this part actually works. SQL has quietly become the shared language of trust between business and engineering. The problem is not the agreement. The problem is the infrastructure and latency that come after it.

In this talk, I explore an emerging data engineering workflow where AI is used not to replace engineers or generate insights, but to automate the most mechanical parts of the analytics lifecycle. Agreed upon SQL becomes the source of truth that defines pipelines, database views, and production ready endpoints automatically. The result is a much shorter path from business logic to usable analytics.

What emerges looks remarkably similar to the communication patterns promised by data mesh. Clear ownership, faster access to analytics, and tighter collaboration between business and engineering. The difference is that this is achieved through workflow automation rather than organizational redesign.

This talk is about rethinking the operational flow of data engineering. It focuses on where humans should stay deeply involved, where automation actually helps, and why the most impactful applications of AI are often the least flashy.

sessions.session_title: From Prediction to Action: Building an Enterprise AI Platform that Drives Real-World Decisions
sessions.session_abstract:
This session presents a practical, end-to-end blueprint for building an enterprise AI platform that transforms predictions into timely, high-impact decisions. Using healthcare readmissions as a guiding example, Jeff will demonstrate how to unify diverse data sources, from structured records to real-time event streams, into a governed, AI-ready foundation.
Attendees will learn how to design scalable data pipelines, implement reusable feature layers, and apply proven modeling patterns such as risk scoring and anomaly detection while addressing challenges like data drift and class imbalance. The session concludes with strategies for operationalizing insights through role-based analytics, enabling organizations to move from experimentation to measurable business outcomes.

sessions.session_title: Beyond Transcripts: Configurable Voice Intelligence You Can Plug In Anywhere
sessions.session_abstract: Most voice AI systems flatten audio into text and hand it to a language model — losing the nuance that actually matters. In this demo session, I introduce Velma, the world's first production Ensemble Listening Model (ELM). I'll show how you can connect any audio source — live streams or recorded files — and configure Velma to provide voice-native insights in real-time on all aspects of the conversation: including transcriptions, emotion detection, deepfake detection, and other signals, as well as recognizing higher-level patterns such as fraud, policy violations, customer sentiment, or entirely custom behaviors. The same platform that catches refund abuse for a food delivery company also detects voice manipulation in banking calls — no retraining, just configuration. Under the hood, an ensemble of specialized sub-models delivers state-of-the-art accuracy at a fraction of foundation model compute costs. Attendees will leave with a concrete understanding of how to integrate real-time voice intelligence into their own pipelines and products.

sessions.session_title: Coffee Break
Book Author:TECH CONFIDENTIAL: The Insider's Playbook for Daring Entrepreneurs
The Spectrum of Agentic Coding: From Vibe Coding to High-quality Software Engineering
sessions.session_abstract:
After spending over a billion tokens on AI-assisted coding over the past two and a half years - from creating one of the first agentic coding tools (Kaguya) to daily production use - I've realized that vibe coding and traditional software engineering are not opposites. They exist on a spectrum.

This talk introduces a four-level framework for agentic coding:

1. Vibe coding - let AI go wild, forget the code exists
2. Agentic coding with discipline - version control, file-level understanding, basic testing
3. Agentic software engineering - CI, pre-commit hooks, function-level understanding
4. High-quality software engineering - line-by-line understanding, interactive code reviews, indistinguishable from handwritten code

sessions.session_title: Beyond the black box: Interpretability of LLMs in FInance
sessions.session_abstract: As finance adopts LLMs, the need for transparency is more critical than ever. Mechanistic interpretability acts as "neuroscience for AI" to reveal internal reasoning and make black-box models transparent. We will practically demonstrate how to use Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to extract hidden trading features and pinpoint credit risk concepts for targeted sentiment analysis. Finally, an end-to-end case study will showcase a clear before-and-after of controlling hallucinations. By isolating and suppressing the model's internal features, we will demonstrate exactly how to transform a fabricated financial claim into a strictly factual, reliable output.

sessions.session_title: Reinforcement Learning for LLM
sessions.session_abstract: Reinforcement learning has become ubiquitous with LLM model training ever since RLHF was a thing. Initially, it was only used for teaching the model to behave, but over time people have found out that it's a very good way to increase the model's performance on verifiable tasks like math and code. A lot of practitioners might be familiar with supervised fine-tuning. Reinforcement Learning is a different beast with its own challenges. We would like to focus on how this differs from supervised fine tuning and why it is better or worse, and how to potentially address these challenges. In the talk, we would like to focus on a notebook that we designed to make the model learn to play a game using reinforcement learning. This is aimed at people who are both trying to get their hands dirty with reinforcement learning and model training, and also people who want to squeeze out the last bits of performance from their models.

sessions.session_title: Using Personal AI in 2026 (OpenClaw)
sessions.session_abstract:
This session will teach you how personal AI, namely OpenClaw, can drastically increase your productivity. We'll walk through the set up of KiloClaw (cloud-hosted, fully managed OpenClaw), plus 4 real use cases that you can set up to offload 2 hours of work per day.
You'll walk away with a fundamental understanding of personal AI, plus practical workflows that we'll spin up together, live.

sessions.session_title: Migrating to the Lakehouse: Incremental Strategies Using Query Federation
sessions.session_abstract:
In this interactive workshop, Alex Merced guides participants through the fundamentals of the data lakehouse and shows how to migrate toward it incrementally using query federation. The session is designed to be practical and applied, focusing on real architectural decisions rather than theory.

Participants will work through common migration scenarios where existing data warehouses, operational databases, and data lakes must continue operating while new lakehouse capabilities are introduced. Alex will demonstrate how query federation creates a unified analytical layer across systems, enabling teams to shift workloads gradually without disrupting users or downstream tools.

By the end of the workshop, attendees will understand how to identify good candidate workloads for migration, design a phased transition plan, and avoid common pitfalls such as duplicated logic, broken governance, and unnecessary data movement. This workshop is ideal for data engineers, architects, and technical leaders who want a clear, hands-on blueprint for moving to a lakehouse without a risky “all at once” migration.

sessions.session_title: The Verifier–Compiler Loop: Turning Human Preferences into Production Agent Judgment
sessions.session_abstract:
Agents can look great in a demo and still fail in production—especially once they're multi‑turn, tool‑using, and operating under real policies, risk limits, and customer expectations. As organizations deploy more agents across workflows, the problem gets sharper: each additional handoff, tool call, or sub‑agent multiplies the ways agents can fail, and small per‑step error rates compound into major business impact.
This session argues that the fastest path to reliability is not "better models," but better judgment: explicitly capturing how your organization wants agents to behave when goals conflict (speed vs certainty, customer empathy vs strict compliance, containment vs escalation). We'll walk through a practical, production-ready pattern: a human‑governed verifier–compiler that continuously verifies agent behavior against expert standards and compiles expert decisions into a versioned behavioral contract—the Options Model—with two layers: Skills (how work is executed) and Judgment (global boundaries, priorities, quality bars, and escalation rules).
You'll learn how the full pipeline works: observe real traces and outcomes, evaluate with purpose-built evaluators and a reasoning judge, intervene safely at runtime (rewrite/route/slow‑halt/escalate), and ship week‑over‑week improvements

sessions.session_title: Building an AI Research Copilot with Hybrid Search and Knowledge Graphs
sessions.session_abstract:
Modern scientific research generates enormous volumes of literature, making it increasingly difficult for researchers to discover relevant information efficiently. PubMed alone contains more than 39 million abstracts, and traditional keyword-based search often fails to surface the most relevant findings.

In this hands-on workshop, participants will build a research copilot that combines vector search (Qdrant) with knowledge graphs (Neo4j) to explore biomedical literature more effectively. The system uses hybrid retrieval and an AI agent to interpret user intent, dynamically selecting the best search strategy and enriching results with structured knowledge.

Attendees will walk through the architecture and interact with a working system that demonstrates how vector search and knowledge graphs together enable more powerful discovery workflows.


Session learning objectives:
- Understand the limitations of traditional keyword search in scientific research.
- Learn how hybrid search combines semantic retrieval and structured querying.
- Understand how vector databases and knowledge graphs complement each other.
- Gain practical insight into designing agentic retrieval systems.
- Leave with a clear architectural pattern for building AI-powered research copilots

sessions.session_title: Distributed Training with PyTorch and Ray: Fine-Tuning Robot Policies at Scale
sessions.session_abstract: In this talk you will learn how to scale ML workloads with PyTorch and Ray, beyond the limitations of a single node and GPU. Vision Language Action (VLA) models are at the frontier of embodied AI, enabling robots to interpret language instructions and act in the physical world. But fine-tuning a 7B-parameter VLA on robot trajectory data is computationally demanding and doing it efficiently requires rethinking your training infrastructure. With Ray Train and PyTorch FSDP, we will show how to distribute VLA fine-tuning across heterogeneous GPU clusters with minimal code changes. By the end of this session, you'll walk away with reusable patterns for distributed fine-tuning, an intuition for where Ray fits in the robotics ML stack, and a ready-to-run template you can adapt for your own robot learning workflows.

sessions.session_title: The Sovereign Stack: Why Private LLMs are the Only Path to Strategic Independence in 2026
sessions.session_abstract: For years, enterprises relied on third-party foundation models, often trading data privacy and strategic alignment for ease of access. In 2026, that trade-off is no longer necessary or defensible. As foundation model providers increasingly bake specific agendas and commercial biases into their "black box" systems, organizations face a critical choice: remain a tenant on someone else’s platform or build their own intelligence. This session explores the shift from massive capital expenditure to high-efficiency deployment. We will examine how the maturation of the open-source ecosystem has democratized high-performance LLMs, allowing companies to fortify their market position through custom-tuned, private models. Attendees will learn how to leverage these "off-the-shelf" giants to create a proprietary competitive advantage without the billion-dollar price tag.

sessions.session_title: Coffee Break
Agentic Data Engineering You Can Trust
sessions.session_abstract:
Generating data pipelines takes minutes with modern coding agents, but moving from "cool demo" to production-grade data platform requires solving for reliability, consistency, security, and "Day 2" operations. This intermediate-level workshop focuses on the transition from agentic prototypes to trustworthy automation. 

Practitioners will go from zero to a production-grade data system in a single session: designing a data model, generating synthetic data, building analytics pipelines, and exposing secured APIs. Every artifact is autonomously generated with AI, built on open source, and hardened to meet enterprise security and scalability requirements.
We introduce three feedback mechanisms that systematically improve agent-built data systems:
World Models for Data Manipulation: Provides guard-rails to agents that produce higher-quality results.


Validation and Optimization: Constraining LLM flexibility with rule-based determinism to ensure consistent output.


Dataflow Simulation: Accurately simulate real-world data flows so the agent can iterate toward correct solutions.
You will apply all three live, building and hardening an MCP server for Customer 360 access and navigating the real world complexities of data integration, transformation, and secured data access.
The full stack runs on Apache Iceberg, Flink, Kafka, and other open-source tools you can take back to your organization without vendor lock-in or licensing conversations and  that participants can run locally. 
For the coding agent only, participants need access to Claude Code or similar coding agents.

sessions.session_title: Agents in the wild: Shipping a CI triage agent you can actually debug
sessions.session_abstract:
CI failures are noisy. The fix is buried. Most teams end up grepping logs manually when they could have an agent do it for them.

In this live demo, we build a pipeline failure triage agent that triggers on CI failures, pulls relevant Kubernetes pod logs, proposes a fix, and opens a GitHub issue with an actionable summary. The agent is built with LangChain and LlamaStack, which handles model and tool connectivity, safety controls, and end-to-end telemetry via OpenTelemetry.

But the real talk is about what happens after you ship the agent. Agentic systems drift. Prompts evolve, tools change, models get swapped, and small updates quietly break behavior. We'll show how to treat your agent like any other release artifact: build it, evaluate it, roll it out safely, and catch regressions before users do, using Kubeflow Pipelines and model evals.

We'll intentionally introduce a regression, watch the pipeline catch it, and use traces to pinpoint exactly what changed. You'll leave with a practical, upstream-first pattern for shipping agentic automation that stays reliable as it grows.

sessions.session_title: What Does the Future of Software Look Like?
sessions.session_abstract:
Software is no longer static. It’s no longer the same for everyone.
We’re entering a new era where every instance of software behaves differently, adapting to the individual using it. Systems learn how you work—such as how you prefer to be notified, what matters, and when to act—and continuously reshape without ever hitting a deploy button.

This shift introduces a fundamental change: software is becoming non-deterministic. The same input may produce different outputs. Behavior evolves over time. Memory compounds. Replayability and trust become first-class problems, no longer edge cases.

At the same time, the explosion of specialized, role-oriented AI tools has created a new challenge: fragmentation. Developers hack their way across systems. Non-technical teams struggle to keep up. The real value no longer lives in any single tool, but in how systems work together.

This is where a new layer emerges: software as an adaptive integration fabric—a system that orchestrates tools, learns from behavior, and continuously rebuilds itself around the user.

But making this real is hard.

How do you manage unpredictability in LLM-driven systems?

How do you ensure consistency, replayability, and trust?

How do you control costs as models like Anthropic’s Claude and modern coding agents unlock exponentially more capability, but at real expense?

In this session, we’ll explore how software is shifting from deterministic tools to adaptive systems and what it means to build, operate, and scale in a world where software is no longer fixed, but alive.

sessions.session_title: Agents Unchained: The Case For (and Against) Harnessing Multi-Agent Systems
sessions.session_abstract:
Multi-agent systems are the most exciting development in AI since ChatGPT. The vision is irresistible: specialized agents collaborating autonomously to handle complex workflows end-to-end. And the early results are real - enterprises are deploying multi-agent architectures for sales development, document processing, and customer operations with measurable ROI. But the gap between demo and production is wider than most teams expect, and the industry is learning this the hard way.

OpenClaw became one of the fastest-growing open-source repos in GitHub history by betting on autonomy first, safety second. Users reported extraordinary productivity gains - and then one agent deleted a Meta researcher's entire inbox, ignoring explicit stop commands. The guardrails it had were configurable but brittle: prompt-level instructions that got lost during context compaction. It's the perfect case study for why multi-agent systems need what the industry is now calling a "harness" - architectural constraints that cannot be overridden by the agent's own reasoning.

But here's the counterpoint: too much harness kills the value proposition. The art is calibrating autonomy to earned trust. In this talk, I'll draw on real enterprise case studies from my work and my book Building Agentic AI to show what's actually working in production multi-agent deployments, where teams are getting burned, and the specific engineering patterns - evaluation loops, progressive autonomy, cost controls, and human-in-the-loop gates - that separate systems you can trust from systems you have to babysit. You'll leave with a practical framework for deciding how much harness your agents actually need.

sessions.session_title: Building Effective Agentic Systems
sessions.session_abstract: This session explores practical patterns for designing and deploying effective agentic AI systems with CrewAI, showing how to turn large language models into reliable, production-grade workflows by focusing less on model choice and more on controlling inputs, scoping tasks, and orchestrating agents and tools.

sessions.session_title: Coffee Break
Panel: Investing in the Future: What’s Next for AI and Tech Startups?
sessions.session_abstract: In this panel discussion, seasoned experts, investors, and visionaries will come together to shed light on the emerging trends, ecosystems, and disruptive technologies that are fostering the growth of the next generation of AI startups. Discover the secrets to recognizing innovation, and gain insights into the unique qualities that set these startups on a path to success. Be part of the conversation that’s shaping the future of AI entrepreneurship and innovation.
sessions.full_name:
Ali Mahmoud | 
Mike Obolonskyi | Nilanjana Bhowmik | Erkang Zheng
sessions.company:
Glasswing Ventures | 
Cortical Ventures | Converge | Ariso.ai

sessions.session_title: Using Model Context Protocol to Support Agentic Behaviors in your Applications
Deep Dive on Deep Agents
sessions.session_abstract:
This session introduces Deep Agents, an open-source agent harness built on LangChain and LangGraph that brings professional-grade agentic capabilities to your Python applications.

Deep Agents implements three core principles seen in agent harnesses like Claude Code: structured planning (before task execution), computer access (filesystem and shell operations), and sub-agent delegation (for isolated, parallel task execution).

In this session, we'll explore:

The middleware architecture that powers Deep Agents' built-in tools (todo management, file operations, sub-agent spawning)

How to extend agents with custom models, tools, and instructions

Practical patterns for human-in-the-loop workflows and long-term memory

I'm looking forward to having you join me on this deep dive into deep agents!

sessions.session_title: How do you go from idea to production-ready AI company in months—not years?
sessions.session_abstract:
In this 30-minute live demo, super{set}, a venture builder focused on AI-native enterprise companies, opens the playbook behind its Signal program, where founders partner with experienced operators to rapidly validate, build, and launch new products.
We begin with the foundation: how open source infrastructure like Majordomo (super{set}’s internal control plane) similar to OpenRouter helps teams manage LLM sprawl, optimize inference costs, and orchestrate workflows across multiple models and providers, while offering them full control and the backing of open source community. As AI systems scale, controlling cost, performance, and governance becomes as critical as model quality itself.
From there, we move from infrastructure to application.
You’ll see how one company was built to solve a growing problem in AI development: how to continuously test and validate AI-generated code. Through a live walkthrough of Checksum, we’ll demonstrate how super{set} designed systems that automatically generate, execute, and evolve tests alongside code, dramatically reducing QA bottlenecks and enabling faster, safer iteration in production environments.
This session is designed for builders, engineers, and founders looking to understand not just how to use AI, but how to build durable, scalable companies around it.
What you’ll learn:
How super{set}’s Signal program identifies and builds AI-native companies


Why LLM control planes are becoming essential infrastructure for modern AI stacks


How to manage cost, performance, and orchestration across models using open source tools


A live demo of continuous testing for AI-generated code using Checksum


Practical patterns for moving from prototype to production in AI-native systems

sessions.session_title: Sub-500ms Voice/Video AI: Global Architecture for Cost & Latency
sessions.session_abstract:
In this session, we will provide a live demonstration on how the world’s most advanced AI workloads from Tavus, Deepgram, Livekit, and Resemble AI achieve sub-500ms end-to-end interactions globally, through tight co-location of the full pipeline. How serverless GPUs and region-aware orchestration let you absorb bursty traffic without over-provisioning. And how self-hosted production-grade deployments can improve reliability and unit economics at scale. Ultimately enabling enterprise level efficiencies for latency, data residency, compliance, and cost.

A reference architecture running voice agents under 500ms within the Cerebrium platform will be provided for autoscaling within a region in a repeatable pattern. Where those same voice agents can then expand cross-regionally, while still adhering to optimization standards & new sovereignty requirements. All without duplicating effort or rewriting your code stack.

sessions.session_title: Entity Resolved Knowledge Graphs: The Foundation for Effective GraphRAG
sessions.session_abstract:
Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) has emerged as one of the most effective strategies for minimizing hallucinations in large language models, and increasingly, practitioners are turning to knowledge graphs rather than simple vector embeddings to power their RAG systems.  This approach, known as GraphRAG, promises more accurate and contextually rich responses by leveraging the structured relationships within knowledge graphs.  However, like all data science solutions, knowledge graphs are subject to the fundamental principle of "garbage in, garbage out."

This hands-on workshop demonstrates why entity resolution is not optional for GraphRAG implementations.  Participants will work with real world data from multiple public sources to compare the performance of RAG systems built on both unresolved tabular data and entity resolved knowledge graphs.  Through practical exercises, attendees will see how variations in business names, addresses, and identifiers can cause even straightforward queries to produce incomplete or incorrect answers when entities are not properly resolved.

The workshop covers the complete pipeline from data ingestion through entity resolution to knowledge graph construction and LLM integration.  Participants will use the open-source Senzing package for industrial grade entity resolution, open-source Python packages for graph database management, and various open-source Python LLM packages for building question answering systems.  Through hands-on coding exercises, attendees will observe how entity resolution enables LLMs to traverse related entities and aggregate information that would otherwise remain siloed in separate, seemingly unconnected records.

By the end of this workshop, participants will understand both the technical implementation details and have empirical evidence that entity resolution is fundamental infrastructure for production quality GraphRAG systems.  All code and setup instructions will be provided in a GitHub repository via Docker containers and Jupyter notebooks for easy replication.

sessions.session_title: From Model Security to Mission Security: Why AI Fails in 2026
sessions.session_abstract:
By 2026, the most damaging AI security failures will not come from poisoned models or clever prompt injections. They will come from MCP sprawl, a quiet expansion of missions, tools, permissions, and agent trust that no one explicitly approved.

The real risk in AI systems is no longer what a model knows, but what the mission allows it to do.

As enterprises deploy autonomous and semi-autonomous agents, traditional security assumptions break down:

Privileges are inherited silently

Context replaces identity

Agents trust each other implicitly

Logs record actions but lose intent

This session introduces a Mission Security lens for AI shifting the focus from model centric controls to authorization, intent, and irreversible impact.

Attendees will explore the hard truths behind MCP driven risk, including silent privilege creep, context without accountability, agent to agent lateral movement, and why most existing logging and detection systems fail AI environments.



The talk concludes with a practical 2026-ready framework, covering:

Mission based authorization instead of blanket agent access

Just in time AI privileges with automatic expiry

Context integrity scoring before execution

Explainable, auditable action chains 

The evolution from SBOM to MBOM (Mission Bill of Materials)

Grounded in an enterprise consumer perspective and informed by conversations with founders and product leaders actively building AI security platforms, this session offers a realistic, forward looking blueprint for securing AI systems before the breach looks “authorized.

sessions.session_title: Operationalizing Data Contracts: From Governance to Execution
Outclassing Frontier LLMs at Extracting Information
sessions.session_abstract:
Accurately extracting information from documents has been a decades-old dream. Important workflows — from automated back-office processing to enterprise RAG — depend on it.

LLMs promise to fulfill this dream but currently fall short: they hallucinate information, struggle with long documents, break down on complex layouts, and are often too big to be used privately. The solution: Small Language Models (SLMs) specialized in information extraction.

In this talk, I will present various SLMs specialized in extracting information from documents (both for structured-data extraction and for OCR). These open-source models are able to outperform frontier LLMs of OpenAI/Anthropic/Google of while being orders of magnitude smaller, enabling private usage.

I will demonstrate these LLMs abilities, explain how to use them, how they are created, and discuss the future of information extraction.

sessions.session_title: AI Career Fair
sessions.session_abstract: Meet leading AI companies, explore open roles, and have real conversations about your next career step

sessions.session_title: The Leadership Imperative: AI Governance as Strategic Infrastructure
sessions.session_abstract: Most organizations are setting in place AI governance programs that fail to equip them to navigate the implications of the technology they seek to govern. This session is for executives and board members who want to understand why the management and oversight of AI feels different from what they have been asked to account for prior, and what is required to position an organization to its advantage.

sessions.session_title: How Global Enterprise AI is Solving for Cost Efficiency & Latency with Cerebrium
sessions.session_abstract:
AI models are getting smarter across language, voice, vision, and multimodal tasks accelerating them into business workflow adoption. Resulting in a shift of commercial vision changing from “can we use AI?” to “how do we integrate AI systems reliably and economically at scale?” 

This session will show how the world’s most advanced AI workloads from Tavus, Deepgram, Livekit, and Resemble AI have solved that question achieving sub-500ms globally and compliantly with optimized latency, efficient cost structures, and compliant data residency — through Cerebrium.

A live demonstration of the reference architecture will run voice agents, autoscaling within a region which can then expand across new regions maintaining the same optimizations, without requiring rewritten code stacks or management from development teams.

sessions.session_title: Verification-Driven Agentic Workflows
sessions.session_abstract:
We are in the midst of a fundamental change in how work gets done. Workflow automation has transformed—and continues to transform—software engineering, and is increasingly being applied to broader and broader fields. At this moment, sentiment around the quality of results produced by automated, agentic work is incredibly varied.

Within software engineering, we’re seeing a rise in codebases that “work” but are increasingly brittle and difficult to validate: generated faster than they can be understood, over-abstracted, and hard to debug when things go wrong. 

This talk will introduce a framing and an approach to designing workflows that bring rigor and best practices from the relevant field into the workflows being automated. The examples examined will focus on software engineering, but the ideas are conceptually applicable to any field of work where an open-ended job to be done can be automated by a workflow—a range that is increasing rapidly.

As a teaser, we’ll draw on recent research and practices in the ML space that support the models proposed in this talk.

sessions.session_title: Everyone deserves to enjoy Video Games: using Visual Language Models for accessibility
sessions.session_abstract:
My friend's daughter loves Stardew Valley. But the way her vision works, she can't see the items clearly when they appear in treasure chests or her inventory. I wanted to help her enjoy playing the game rather than getting frustrated trying to figure out what goodies were in the treasure chest. Turns out, advances in Vision Language Models (VLMs) make this a tractable problem I could take on.
We built a simple pipeline: she takes a screenshot on her iPad, sends it to a visual language model, gets back a text description of what's on screen, and hears it read aloud. Suddenly those treasure chests made sense. The game became less frustrating for her.
This talk is about how you can take what she and I learned to leverage the power of Visual Language Models for other use cases. It's about how to use these tools to expand accessibility in new and exciting ways.
In this session, we'll explore what visual language models actually are and why they're powerful for this kind of problem. We'll walk through our specific implementation: the decisions we made, the models we tested, and why some worked better than others. Most importantly, you'll see the working application and dig into the actual code. We'll talk through what we learned—what surprised us, what didn't work, and where we hit the edge cases.
By the end, you'll understand not just how we solved this accessibility problem, but the shape of similar problems you might solve. You'll leave seeing it in action, a Github repo of code, and a HuggingFace repo for our models which means you can go find your own applications of this work.
Come see how visual AI can expand what's possible for the people using the software you build.

sessions.session_title: Real-Time AI Risk Monitoring in Healthcare
sessions.session_abstract:
As AI systems become increasingly integrated into pharmaceutical research and clinical decision-making, ensuring their safety, reliability, and regulatory compliance has become paramount. This session addresses the critical need for comprehensive AI risk monitoring frameworks specifically designed for healthcare applications, where model failures can have life-threatening consequences.

Attendees will learn to implement production-grade monitoring systems that detect model drift, data quality issues, and fairness violations in real-time. We'll explore practical strategies for monitoring AI systems deployed in back-office and diagnostic support tools. The session covers both technical implementation and regulatory considerations, including FDA guidance on AI/ML-based medical devices and EU MDR requirements.

Through hands-on examples using real-world healthcare datasets, participants will build monitoring dashboards that track model performance metrics, detect distribution shifts in patient populations, and identify potential biases across demographic groups. We'll demonstrate how to create automated alerting systems that trigger when models operate outside safe parameters, ensuring timely intervention before patient care is compromised.

Target audience includes ML engineers, data scientists, and clinical informaticians working in pharmaceutical companies, hospitals, and health tech organizations who need to ensure their AI systems meet both performance and regulatory standards.

sessions.session_title: How I learned to Stop Worrying and love AI Regulation
sessions.session_abstract:
Despite rumors to the contrary, AI regulation is happening. States like California, Colorado and Kentucky (if you can believe it) are moving forward with their own regulation strategies. And of course there's the minor factor of 1/6 of the global economy being locked behind compliance with the EU AI Act.

But don't panic! This isn't 50 states with 50 policies; there are common requirements in these regulations so that coming into compliance with one likely means others are covered. Regulators are mainly focused on processes being observable, data being traceable and outputs being accountable. Even better? This is just good machine learning practice. Amazon's "Well-Architected" framework pays special attention to model and data lineage and Google warns that lack of transparency creates major risks in ML pipelines.

In this talk we'll explore some key AI regulations and how their requirements overlap with machine learning best practices.  Then, we'll review useful tools (e.g. IBM Fairness 360) and approaches (e.g. model cards) to meeting these requirements. I'll wrap up with a real world example of implementing an AI Management System and how it sped up iteration and feature development.

sessions.session_title: How to provide Data Science Teams with AI-Ready Data Infrastructure
sessions.session_abstract: AI-ready data infrastructure gives ML teams faster access to data, reproducible experiments, built-in quality gates, and compliance audit trails, without requiring them to change how they work. But deploying the infrastructure is the one thing; getting data scientists and ML engineers to actually use it is a different challenge. This session will demonstrate how governed and reproducible data workflows can integrate into familiar tools like Jupyter, MLflow, and Airflow to eliminate data copy wait times, improve data quality, and enhance reproducibility and compliance, and discusses how to reinforce adoption through smart defaults, visibility, and team culture.

sessions.session_title: What "AI-Ready Data" Actually Means: A Framework to Trustworthy AI
sessions.session_abstract:
Teams are being asked to trust AI systems with real-world decisions before their data layer is ready. In analytics, bad data produces a wrong chart. In AI, bad data produces bad decisions at runaway speed and scale.

This talk introduces the AI-Ready Data Framework, an open standard for assessing whether data is ready for AI consumption. I’ll cover 6 factors of AI-ready data and show how they apply differently to RAG, agents, feature serving, and training workloads.
Then, I’ll demonstrate how the framework becomes practical through measurable requirements that can help you assess AI readiness, prioritize gaps, and plan investments.
You will leave with:

A concrete framework for evaluating AI-readiness claims, including how to pressure-test your own and 3rd party claims
A shared vocabulary to align data, ML, and product teams on what "AI-ready" means
A clear picture of how readiness requirements differ across RAG, agents, and training workloads 
A practical model for deciding what to fix before trusting AI with real decisions

sessions.session_title: Agents Don't Need Better Frameworks: They Need a New Runtime
sessions.session_abstract:
Every team building custom agents hits the same wall. LangChain gets you to a demo by Friday. Then production arrives. Agents crash mid-tool-call. Retries double-charge the API. State sprawls across three databases. What started as "let's ship an agent" becomes "let's build distributed systems in YAML."

The industry's answer has been more nodes, more decorators, more DSLs. None of it works. Because frameworks aren't the problem to solve. The runtime is.

This session surveys the two competing approaches to production agents: prompt-chaining frameworks like LangChain and LangGraph, and agent-native runtimes like Rivet, Cloudflare Agents, and Golem. We'll weigh the tradeoffs honestly, then go deep on what we've learned building Golem. My argument: frameworks can't paper over a runtime mismatch, and the workloads agents produce demand primitives no framework can provide. Durable execution. Exactly-once external calls. Instant resume from crashes. Per-agent sandboxing. High-density scheduling. Atomic versioned deploys.

You'll leave able to diagnose whether your current stack is masking a runtime problem, map the landscape with honest tradeoffs, identify the primitives that matter in production, and make an informed architectural call for your team's next agent system. This session is for platform engineers, staff engineers, and tech leads architecting agents for production—especially those hitting the limits of framework-based approaches.

Frameworks made agents possible. Runtimes will make them real!

sessions.session_title: The AI Agent Memory Landscape
sessions.session_abstract: As AI agents evolve from stateless chatbots to persistent, context-aware systems, memory has become the critical differentiator between demos and production-ready applications. This talk maps the rapidly emerging ecosystem of agent memory solutions and provides practical guidance on choosing and implementing the right memory architecture for your agents.

sessions.session_title: Enhancing Context Engineering with Agentic Integration into Vector Database Queries
sessions.session_abstract: Vector databases allow us to store and search through mountains of text in a way that is relevant to our natural way of speaking. If traditional keyword search is a classic roadmap atlas, vector databases are a comprehensive repository of maps from around the world, RAG is a GPS with voice control and agentic integration is your own personal navigation app with history, favorite points of interest and recommendations of places you might want to go for coffee tomorrow morning. Like keyword search and vector databases, RAG is an integral component of the future of context engineering, much of which hinges on vector databases. The vectors store contextual information in a way that allows our data to be queried using context rich phrases. When we query "Why do airplanes fly?" we get an article about the aerodynamics of flight and not a website with an ad about "Why you should fly on Expensive Air's airplanes". However, there are limits to the number of objects that a database can return with each query which can lead to incomplete and incorrect answers to complex queries. For example, the query "Show me products that are eco-friendly, budget-friendly, good for sensitive skin, trending on TikTok, and available for same-day delivery in Austin, TX" is succinct in exactly what the user is looking for. Unfortunately, this query is conceptually broad that is spans multiple semantic areas of the database. There are at least 6 individual semantic components of this query. Even if we return 10 objects from our query, we will most likely still miss a number of objects that we need to generate enough context for our generative model to create a comprehensive return for our query. Ideally, all of our users would have taken a masterclass in vector databases and query construction, but that is unrealistic. Therefore, we need to build in features to mitigate less than desirable queries to aid in optimizing database retrieval. Luckily agents, generative model calls that have specific tools or functionality, can complete specific tasks in our RAG pipeline. With the above query, an agent can rewrite this query into multiple sub-queries, direct clarifying questions back to the user before sending the query to the database, create dynamic filtering to flush out non-semantic components of the query or work in parallel to perform all of the above. In this workshop we will be begin by learning the fundamentals and features of vector databases by using Weaviate and Docker to spin up a local vector database. Then we will work with Pydantic to create custom agents to generate optimized queries and create dynamic filters for enhanced retrieval. We'll also build a lightweight evaluation workflow so you can tell whether your retrieval is actually improving as you add each component. By the end of the workshop you will have an in-depth understanding of vector database functionality, data population, query retrieval and response generation as well as how to create your own production ready agents!

sessions.session_title: From Retrieval to Reasoning: Building AI Agents on Elastic
sessions.session_abstract:
Developers building AI applications want domain-aware agents that combine reasoning, orchestration, and high-quality retrieval across enterprise data. Grounded in Elastic’s Search AI Platform, Agent Builder enables developers to create agents that use hybrid retrieval across vector search, keyword search, and structured filtering to assemble the right context for each task. That matters in real systems, where data is fragmented, noisy, and constantly changing, and where retrieval quality directly impacts the usefulness, accuracy, and reliability of agent outputs.

Built on Elastic’s Search AI capabilities, Agent Builder gives developers a practical framework for moving beyond simple chat interfaces and point solutions. Agents can retrieve from unstructured documents, structured records, and operational data, then apply tools and workflows to investigate, summarize, and act on what they find. It makes it easier to build systems that move from retrieval to reasoning to execution across security, observability, and enterprise search use cases, while staying grounded in relevant, high-quality data and reducing the amount of custom glue code developers have to maintain.

sessions.session_title: The Hidden Data Problem Blocking AI at Scale
sessions.session_abstract:
AI has never been easier to build, yet harder than ever to scale.
 

Why do so many AI initiatives show early promise but struggle to reach production? What’s really slowing teams down behind the scenes?

 
With each new project, teams are forced to:

Rebuild logic
Reapply governance
Revalidate and trust the data feeding their models
 

The result is slow delivery, rising costs, and stalled impact. Is the problem really the models, or the data foundation behind them?

 

In this session, we explore an architectural approach built on:

Governed data access
Shared business meaning
Reusable data products
 

Walk away with a clear blueprint to move from isolated AI wins to enterprise-wide value.

sessions.session_title: Your Agent Should Fix Itself: Agent GPA + Coding Agents = Self-Improving Agents
sessions.session_abstract:
Most enterprise agents don’t fail on the easy cases, they fail on the ones you didn’t plan for.

In this workshop, we’ll show how to systematically find and fix those failures using the Agent Goal-Plan-Action Alignment (Agent GPA) framework, which achieved state-of-the-art results on TRAIL/GAIA with 95% error coverage and 86% error localization.

Paired with coding agents, Agent GPA turns evaluation into a feedback loop that continuously improves your system. By pulling in “hard cases” from production data, you can refine your agent’s instructions and tool use without manual guesswork.

This is a hands-on session. You’ll work with simulated production data to surface real failure modes, then use agent-driven optimization to make the system measurably better by the end of the workshop.

sessions.session_title: Meaningful Data Visualization in the Age of AI
sessions.session_abstract: In this session, we will discuss how to create meaningful data visualizations in the age of AI through a holistic systems engineering approach. As AI quickly evolves with new techniques and tools being released on a daily basis, data visualization can bring rigor and structure to complex, highly interconnected AI systems that easily grow out of control. Engineers and analysts are drowning in massive amounts of seemingly disjointed results. Meaningful data visualization is part of an effective approach to bring context and order to a massive jumble of AI tools, objectives, designs, system management and results. Effective data visualization helps bring the human expert back into the equation: an expert can recognize a previously known pattern, spot emerging patterns, and discover almost-patterns which are difficult to recognize through traditional analytics or AI alone. In addition, we discuss how a systems engineering approach brings more reliability and effectiveness to ever-growing AI system-of-systems.

sessions.session_title: Harness Engineering: Practical Patterns for Agent-First Software Development
sessions.session_abstract:
Reasoning models and coding agents have changed what is possible in software development, but most teams are still using workflows designed for a world where humans write nearly all of the code directly. That mismatch is where a lot of the confusion around AI coding comes from. The strongest results do not come from simply giving a model access to a repository and asking it to start building. They come from designing the surrounding system that makes agent output useful, repeatable, and trustworthy.

This talk introduces Harness Engineering: an agent-first approach to software development where the key engineering task increasingly shifts from manually producing every implementation detail to building the harnesses that let agents do meaningful work safely and at high quality. In practice, that means defining goals clearly, shaping context, constraining tool access, establishing verification and evaluation loops, and building feedback systems that keep agents productive without making them reckless.

I’ll share a practical framework for how to make coding agents actually work in real software environments. We’ll cover how to scope tasks so they are tractable for agents, how to think about context windows and state, how to design acceptance checks and guardrails, and how to identify the kinds of work agents can already do well versus the situations where they still fail badly. I’ll also discuss why raw model capability is not the same thing as deployable engineering output, and why most production success comes from the harness around the model rather than the model alone.

For teams trying to move beyond demos and autocomplete into real agentic software delivery, this talk offers concrete patterns, failure modes, and mental models for building software when humans increasingly steer and agents increasingly execute.

sessions.session_title: Implementing a Self-service Data Platform
sessions.session_abstract:
If you're a data engineer, data platform engineer, or data architect, you know how difficult it is to build a truly self-serve data platform. In fact, data platforms are often organizational bottlenecks, preventing data publication, consumption, and application of data, and by extension, AI.

This means you and your team spends time on low-value work: moving data between systems, managing access policies, and handling endless support tickets.

Instead, the data platform should be an enabler, providing self-service tooling that shifts ownership and responsibility to data producers while freeing your team to unlock data's true value.

In this talk, Andrew explains why the self-service data platform is the foundational layer that abstracts complexity and provides standardized, easy-to-use capabilities. The key architectural feature is the data contract, which provides the context needed to build the platform easily while maximizing user value.

sessions.session_title: Why every AI agent needs a simulation sandbox?
sessions.session_abstract:
AI agents fail in unpredictable ways that traditional testing can't catch (hallucinations, wrong tool calls, policy violations, etc.) Teams only discover these failures after users hit them in production.
A simulation sandbox gives you a controlled environment with realistic users, tools, and workflows where you can run hundreds of scenarios against your agent before it ships, catching edge cases and adversarial inputs that would be impossible to test manually and improve your agent on those.
This talk covers why simulation-driven development is becoming essential infrastructure for any team building production AI agents, and how it closes the gap between "works in demos" and "works at scale.

sessions.session_abstract:

sessions.session_title: Panel: The Future of AI in Healthcare: Trends, Challenges, and Opportunities
sessions.session_abstract: This panel will bring together industry experts to explore the latest trends, key challenges, and future opportunities in AI-driven healthcare. Join us for an insightful discussion on the evolving landscape, ethical considerations, and real-world applications shaping the future of healthcare innovation.
sessions.full_name:
