sessions.session_title: Executive Roundtable: Scaling AI in the Enterprise
Lunch Break
Strategies for Customizing LLM Evaluation at Scale
sessions.session_abstract: LLMs are becoming increasingly common pieces in machine learning applications across different industries. However, even though we know we need to have evaluation processes for machine learning solutions, it can be difficult to figure out how to build LLM evaluation techniques that are customized to our business use cases, trustworthy, and scalable. In this talk, I will apply lessons from multiple sectors to give best practices for testing and implementing LLM-as-a-judge evaluator models, with highlights on designing custom evaluation rubrics, giving attendees the tools necessary to build LLM evaluation frameworks that leave them and their businesses feeling confident about their LLM performance in production. I will also discuss performance and cost concerns that may come along with this kind of work, and how to balance these with the benefits.

sessions.session_title: Evaluating LLM Applications with MLflow
sessions.session_abstract:
AI teams run evaluations but often lack the tools to answer the follow-up questions: Did the model actually get better? Which samples regressed? Is the improvement statistically significant or just noise?

This session walks through building a production evaluation pipeline with MLflow, covering three layers that work together.

First, we connect external evaluation libraries to MLflow using the third-party scorer interface. You will see how Phoenix (hallucination detection), TruLens (groundedness and agent trace evaluation), and Guardrails AI (deterministic safety validators like toxicity and PII detection) plug into mlflow.genai.evaluate() so teams can run safety and quality checks from a single entry point without switching tools.

Second, we look at practical infrastructure decisions: controlling LLM judge parameters (temperature, top_p) for reproducible scoring, managing scorer concurrency to avoid rate limits, and using uv lockfiles to pin model dependencies so environments match between training and serving.

Third, we build a comparison workflow that aligns samples across two evaluation runs by ID, computes per-sample score deltas, and runs statistical significance tests (bootstrap confidence intervals for continuous scores, McNemar's test for binary pass/fail outcomes) to determine whether a model change is a real improvement or within the margin of error.

Attendees will leave with working code for each layer and a clear picture of how to set up evaluation pipelines that go beyond "run eval, check accuracy" to "run eval, compare, and prove the difference matters." This session is aimed at ML engineers, data scientists, and platform teams building or maintaining LLM-powered applications in production.

sessions.session_title: Breaking the Ice: Learning How AI Really Works Through Play
sessions.session_abstract:
AI is moving fast. Yet inside many organizations, there’s a surprising challenge: teams struggle to talk about it.

Engineers understand the models. Executives see the potential. Product teams see new opportunities. But the language of AI—neural networks, machine learning, large language models—often creates confusion rather than alignment.

This interactive workshop introduces a new way to bridge that gap.

Using FuzzNet Labs (https://cybersecgames.com/products/fuzznet-labs), a collaborative board game designed to explain how neural networks and modern AI systems function, participants will explore the fundamentals of machine learning through play. The game transforms complex ideas—like training data, model behavior, and decision pathways—into tangible, interactive experiences that anyone can understand.

Instead of slides or lectures, participants learn by building, experimenting, and discussing AI concepts together. The result is a shared mental model of how AI works beneath the surface.

Because like an iceberg, most of AI’s complexity sits below the waterline. When teams can see what’s happening underneath, conversations become clearer, collaboration improves, and organizations can move forward with confidence.

By the end of the workshop, participants will have a practical and approachable framework for discussing AI across technical and non-technical teams.

sessions.session_title: The AI Skill Flip:  How Smart Professionals are Reinventing work
sessions.session_abstract: As AI rapidly reshapes industries and job roles, professionals face a critical shift: adapting their skills to stay relevant in an increasingly AI-driven world. In this talk, Sheamus McGovern explores the “AI skill flip”—the transition from traditional technical expertise to a new, human-centered approach that emphasizes working with AI rather than competing against it. Drawing on over a decade of experience at the forefront of AI and insights from leading practitioners, he will outline the emerging skills, tools, and mindsets needed to thrive in the age of large language models, agentic systems, and automation. Attendees will gain practical guidance on how to future-proof their careers and harness AI as a powerful collaborator in their work.
sessions.track: AI and the Future of Work Track

sessions.session_title: Polars: A Highly Optimized Dataframe Library
sessions.session_abstract:
Are you interested in learning about a highly optimized data manipulation tool in the Python ecosystem?

Look no further than Polars - a highly optimized dataframe library that allows you to easily manipulate and analyze large datasets.

In this conference talk, we will introduce you to the key features of Polars and compare them to the popular Pandas library.

sessions.session_title: Designing AI Agents Users Will Trust: Evals, Guardrails, and Feedback Loops
sessions.session_abstract:
As AI systems move from copilots to agents that can take action, the hardest problem is no longer model quality — it is trust. In production environments, users must believe that an AI agent will act correctly, safely, and predictably before they allow it to move beyond recommendations. Yet most teams still rely on offline benchmarks and qualitative demos that break down once agents interact with real users, real data, and real consequences.

In this talk, I will share a product and systems-level perspective on how we designed, shipped, and evaluated agentic AI systems in production, focusing on the evaluation mechanisms that actually mattered. Drawing from real-world deployments of AI agents operating over large-scale telemetry and decision systems, I’ll walk through how we moved beyond static model evals toward continuous, user-centered evaluation loops.

We’ll cover:

Why traditional offline evals fail for agentic systems and where they still help

How to design online and human-in-the-loop evaluations that measure action quality, confidence, and failure modes

The guardrails that enabled agents to act safely, including confidence thresholds, reversibility, and scoped autonomy

Feedback loops that turned user corrections, overrides, and hesitation into signals for improving trust and performance

How these evals directly informed product decisions such as rollout strategy, default behaviors, and autonomy levels

I’ll also share surprising customer reactions — from initial skepticism to long-term reliance — and how evaluation data helped us understand when trust was earned, when it was broken, and how to recover it.

Attendees will leave with a practical framework for evaluating agentic AI in the wild, including metrics, system design patterns, and decision checkpoints they can apply to their own products — whether they are building internal tools or user-facing AI systems at scale.

sessions.session_title: Ready for Primetime: Creating the AI-enabled Clinician
sessions.session_abstract:
I’ve spent my career moving between clinical care, health system leadership, and national technology policy. One thing has become very clear: building AI in healthcare is rarely the hard part. Making it useful is.

In this talk, I’ll share what I’ve learned watching AI move from research and pilot programs into real clinical environments, places where decisions are regulated, time-sensitive, and affect people in front of you, not dashboards. I’ll talk honestly about why many well-intentioned AI tools never make it into everyday use, and why others create friction even when the science is sound.

We’ll look at how data science, advanced models, and real-world data streams intersect with clinical workflow, and where they break down. I’ll also offer a practical way to think about “intelligence” in healthcare: when automation helps, when it harms, and when human judgment must remain in charge.

This keynote is for people building AI systems who want them to matter. We’ll focus less on proof-of-concept and more on adoption, trust, governance, and outcomes; the things that ultimately determine whether AI improves care or quietly disappears.

sessions.session_title: The Compounding Power of Connected Data
sessions.session_abstract:
AI only works as well as the context you feed it. Dr. Denise Gosnell shows how graph data models turn organizational misalignment and data silos into connected systems that actually work together. Through Netflix’s “model once, represent everywhere” approach and real-world examples in cybersecurity, developer ecosystems, and personalization, she shows why companies that think in graphs outperform their peers in both innovation and valuation.

AI performance isn’t limited by model size. It’s limited by missing context. Across industries, teams lose billions each year because their data and definitions don’t align. This talk reframes that problem and shows how graph technologies fix it. You’ll see how four graph patterns (neighborhoods, trees, paths, and recommendations) create shared understanding across systems, improve decision quality, and make AI outputs more accurate and explainable.
We’ll look at Netflix’s Unified Data Architecture as a blueprint for solving silos at scale and how companies like Wiz, Recorded Future, and Slack used graph-based design to create compounding business value. You’ll leave knowing how to spot where your own organization is losing context, how to design a “model once, represent everywhere” foundation, and how to quantify the ROI of aligning your data, teams, and decisions around shared context.
By the end, you’ll see why graph-centric companies don’t just build better AI; they build better alignment, faster innovation, and more valuable businesses.

sessions.session_title: Transform Predictions into Actionable, Explainable Decisions using Optimization
sessions.session_abstract:
Making decisions in a complex environment is difficult, and choosing the right tools
can be just as challenging, but it doesn’t have to be. Mathematical optimization lets you move beyond predictions to identify the best decisions possible, given the realities of your system.
During this session, we'll introduce mathematical optimization as the AI built for decision
making with trust, transparency, and explainability at it's core.

sessions.session_title: How to Build a Startup with an Unfair Advantage
sessions.session_abstract:
What does it actually take to turn an idea into an AI company?
At super{set}, startups are built from the ground up in a matter of months through a rapid company-building program called Signal. Engineers and founders work alongside experienced operators to test ideas, build MVPs, and validate real customer problems through tight learning loops.

In this session, we’ll walk through how one such company—Checksum.ai—was created inside super{set}. Inspired by co-founder Gal Vered’s experience managing product and engineering teams, Checksum.ai tackles one of software development’s hardest problems: maintaining software quality through continuous, autonomous testing.
Senior AI Architect Harsh Maheshwari will show how modern builders can accelerate this process using open-source tooling and AI infrastructure, including Majordomo, a control plane for managing AI stacks, similar to OpenRouter.

How to move from idea → MVP → early customers in months
The open-source tools and scaffolding that accelerate AI product development
How to structure rapid experimentation cycles when building AI products
Practical lessons from launching an AI-native startup

If you're an engineer or builder interested in turning ideas into real AI products, this session will show the tools and playbooks that make it possible.

sessions.session_title: Building Adaptive ML Systems: Detecting and Managing Drift in Production
sessions.session_abstract:
As machine learning systems move from proof-of-concept to production environments, data and concept drift consistently emerge as leading causes of silent model degradation. User behavior evolves, upstream data pipelines change, feature distributions shift, and labels arrive with delays - creating conditions where models trained on historical data no longer reflect current patterns. For ML practitioners across industries, building systems that remain reliable under non-stationary conditions has become a critical competency.
This hands-on workshop provides practical frameworks and tools for detecting, diagnosing, and responding to drift in production ML systems. Participants will learn an actionable taxonomy distinguishing data drift, concept drift, prediction drift, and feature-quality drift, with real production examples illustrating how each manifests across different industries and use cases.
We'll cover a concise but effective toolkit for drift detection: summary statistics checks, lightweight statistical tests, divergence metrics, and feature-level drift reporting. The emphasis is on choosing appropriate monitoring strategies without overengineering or reacting to statistical noise that doesn't impact business metrics.
The hands-on portion guides participants through constructing a complete adaptive ML pipeline: simulating realistic drift scenarios, implementing detection mechanisms, generating interpretable alerts for stakeholders, and triggering controlled retraining workflows with proper versioning and guardrails.
Learning Outcomes:
1. Recognize and categorize different types of drift affecting production models
2. Implement lightweight drift detection using standard Python libraries
3. Build automated retraining pipelines with appropriate safeguards
4. Establish decision frameworks for when to retrain vs. when to investigate further

sessions.session_title: AI Workflow Efficiency: How I Accomplished a Year of Content in One Quarter
sessions.session_abstract: Everyone's buying AI tools. Not everyone's getting results. In this session, Charles Verhey breaks down how his content team produced more web pages in Q1 2026 than in all of 2025 — and why that outcome had less to do with AI and more to do with what you feed it. He'll challenge the assumption that a single enterprise AI license will boost efficiency across the board, make the case that with 90,000+ AI platforms on the market your teams need different tools for different jobs, and explain why AI's inherent unpredictability isn't a problem to solve but a reality to manage. The session closes with a reframe on "AI slop": the issue isn't authenticity, it's consistency — and the only way to maintain it at scale is a shared, high-quality data foundation purpose-built for AI workflows.

sessions.session_title: Stop Writing Prompts, Start Optimizing Them: Prompt Optimization with MLflow
sessions.session_abstract:
Prompt engineering is a time-consuming and frustrating process of trial-and-error, especially when done without an evaluation set or structured feedback from domain experts. For an agent that needs to give grounded answers, invoke tools properly, or match specific output formats, prompt quality directly determines whether users get reliable answers or are able to trust the system at all.
This demo session shows how prompt engineering becomes an automated optimization process using MLflow and Databricks. We show how to apply LLM judges to a classic agent use case, how to define an evaluation set, and how to automatically optimize prompts against gold-standard target answers.
Key Takeaways:
How to define an evaluation set and use prebuilt LLM judges — such as correctness, completeness, retrieval metrics, and tool call efficiency — to measure agent quality systematically
How GEPA, a prompt optimization algorithm available in MLflow, uses LLM-driven reflection to optimize prompts against an evaluation set without any human rewriting needed
How to use MLflow to incorporate subject-matter expert feedback into an evaluation set and continuously benchmark and improve prompt quality over time
Whether it’s tuning a single prompt or a complex agentic workflow, this session will equip the audience to turn prompt engineering from a manual process into a measurable optimization loop.

sessions.session_title: From Data to Decisions: Open Enterprise AI with an Autonomous Data Platform
sessions.session_abstract:
This session explores how enterprise teams can move quickly with AI without compromising governance, security, or operational reliability. It presents an enterprise-ready pattern for agentic and LLM-assisted applications that keeps data grounded in the database while remaining open to the broader ecosystem.

Attendees will see how an autonomous AI-enabled data platform supports natural-language-to-insight workflows and task automation on governed enterprise data through SQL and Python interfaces. The session also highlights in-database machine learning capabilities that enable end-to-end workflows and shows how AI services can automate natural language to SQL and retrieval-augmented generation across multiple models and providers.

The discussion connects key elements of openness and portability, including standards-based interfaces for tools and agents, support for open model formats, and flexible model selection and deployment. Attendees will gain insight into how to build AI applications that balance openness with enterprise control through a modern, database-centric architecture.

sessions.session_title: The Data-Resistant Mind: The Psychology Every Data Scientist Needs to Make Their Work Matter
sessions.session_abstract:
Every experienced data scientist has lived this: a rigorous analysis, clean methodology, surprising and genuinely useful finding - presented to stakeholders, politely received, and quietly ignored. A month later the organization keeps doing exactly what it did before. The common explanation is "they just didn't get it." The real explanation is that human minds (and the organizations made of them) are fundamentally wired to resist evidence that challenges intuition or the status quo. Science has documented over 180 cognitive biases that systematically deflect, minimize, or reframe uncomfortable data. Better charts will not fix this. Better psychology will.
This session is for technical practitioners - data scientists, ML engineers, analysts - who are excellent at the modeling but regularly frustrated by how little of it lands. We'll walk through the specific psychological mechanisms that cause data-resistant behavior in organizations: confirmation bias, the Lake Wobegon effect (everyone thinks biases apply to other people), the false expectation that "the data will speak for itself," and the deep asymmetry between how technical and non-technical colleagues process uncertainty and probability. We'll look at real cases where technically excellent work failed and diagnose what went wrong at the human layer.
Attendees will leave with concrete, immediately usable techniques: a problem-discovery question set that surfaces the real problem before you build the wrong solution; a framework for identifying stakeholders, their incentives, and their hidden resistance from Day 1; communication patterns for presenting probability and uncertainty to executives without triggering the "so what should we do?" reflex; and practical ways to create the psychological safety needed for evidence to actually change minds. We'll also cover how to partner with non-technical colleagues without either oversimplifying your work or drowning them in detail.
Drawn from the framework in my book Data Inspired (Georgetown University Press, 2026) and two decades of leading data projects across Fortune 500s and startups. No statistical background required, this one is about the humans.

sessions.session_title: Real-Time Event-Time Consistent Analytics Pipelines using Kafka, Flink, and Apache Pinot
sessions.session_abstract:
Real-time decision systems increasingly depend on complex data pipelines that must deliver fresh, reliable insights within seconds. Whether powering trading platforms, fraud detection, operational analytics, or user-facing dashboards, organizations now expect analytical systems to handle massive event streams with both low latency and high correctness. Yet, while technologies such as Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, and Apache Pinot have become foundational components of modern data architectures, the industry still lacks a unified model for ensuring consistency and correctness across the full end-to-end pipeline.

This session introduces a comprehensive framework for achieving event-time-consistent, exactly-once real-time analytics across Kafka→Flink→Pinot pipelines. Drawing from real production challenges encountered at scale—including late-arriving events, reprocessing and replay, ingestion backpressure, and multi-tenant OLAP workloads—the presentation identifies the fundamental sources of metric drift and inconsistency within current streaming + OLAP architectures. Attendees will learn how to apply event-time watermarks beyond the streaming layer and into the OLAP ingestion process, enabling predictable completeness windows and preventing partial or inconsistent aggregates from surfacing in dashboards.

The session also presents an ingestion protocol that combines deterministic segment assignment, idempotent upserts, and Flink checkpoint alignment to ensure replay-safe correctness. The result is a system that reduces aggregate divergence, smooths ingestion latency, and improves reliability under real-world workloads.

Participants will walk away with actionable architectural patterns, correctness models, and practical implementation strategies that can be deployed in any organization building real-time analytical systems. This talk bridges the gap between streaming theory and OLAP practice, offering a blueprint for the next generation of trustworthy, high-fidelity real-time analytics.

sessions.session_title: TinyTroupe: Enhancing Marketing Insights through LLM-Powered Multiagent Persona Simulation
sessions.session_abstract:
Understanding customer behavior is essential in marketing. Traditionally, marketers rely on methods such as surveys, customer interviews, and focus groups to gather insights. However, these approaches can be expensive, time-consuming, and limited in scale and diversity.
Recently, multi-agent simulation powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) is emerging as an innovative technique. TinyTroupe, for example, enables the creation of different personas (e.g., budget‑minded Gen‑Z shoppers, premium‑seeking parents), allowing marketers to predict and optimize advertising effectiveness or replace time-consuming interviews rapidly.
In this talk, I will introduce the key concepts of LLM-powered multi-agent simulations, demonstrate their practical application in marketing through TinyTroupe, and share actionable insights and recommendations.

sessions.session_title: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
sessions.session_abstract:
Large language models now require 100x more compute than they did just a few years ago. For most practitioners, this means running and adapting these models is prohibitively expensive — or simply impossible on available hardware. Quantization offers a way out: by reducing the numerical precision of model weights from 16-bit floats to 4-bit integers, we can shrink memory requirements by 4x while preserving most of the model's capability.

This session walks through the mechanics of quantization from first principles. We cover how precision reduction works at the level of individual weights, why naive approaches fail at scale due to emergent outlier features in large models, and how techniques like block-wise quantization and information-theoretic data types solve these problems. We also examine the tradeoff between model size and bit precision — showing when it is better to use a larger model at lower precision versus a smaller model at full precision.

The second half focuses on finetuning quantized models. Directly training in 4-bit destroys model quality, but QLoRA (Quantized Low-rank Adaptation) sidesteps this by freezing quantized weights and training small 16-bit adapter layers. This lets you finetune a 65B-parameter model on a single 48GB GPU with no loss in accuracy compared to full 16-bit finetuning.

Attendees will leave with a practical understanding of when and how to apply quantization for inference and finetuning, what precision levels work for which model sizes, and how to evaluate trade-offs between memory, speed, and quality in their own deployments.

sessions.session_title: A Practical Introduction to Agentic AI
sessions.session_abstract:
Agentic AI is moving beyond chatbots into systems that can plan, decide, and act on their own. But much of the conversation around agents feels abstract or overly complex. This talk offers a practical, hands-on introduction to Agentic AI, focusing on how these systems actually work and how you can start building them today.

We’ll break down core concepts like agents, tools, memory, and orchestration using simple, real-world examples rather than theory-heavy diagrams. You’ll see how agentic workflows differ from traditional prompt-based applications, where they add real value, and where they don’t. The session will also cover common design patterns, pitfalls to avoid, and how to think about reliability, control, and evaluation when agents are allowed to take actions.

By the end of this talk, attendees will have a clear mental model of Agentic AI, understand when it makes sense to use it, and walk away with practical guidance they can apply to real projects immediately.

sessions.session_title: Introduction to the Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol
sessions.session_abstract: This event provides a deep dive into the Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol, equipping engineers with the architectural standards and implementation strategies needed to build interoperable, autonomous systems where AI agents securely discover, negotiate, and collaborate on complex tasks.

sessions.session_title: Training the Best Agentic Open-Source LLMs with Reinforcement Learning
sessions.session_abstract:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a practical and essential tool for training large language models (LLMs) that perform well in real-world applications. While large-scale pretraining establishes broad language capabilities, RL is increasingly crucial for improving instruction following, reasoning quality, and alignment with user and business objectives—making it a critical component of modern LLM production pipelines.

This talk begins with a concise, practitioner-oriented overview of reinforcement learning for LLMs, including why approaches such as GRPO are used to train models for agentic system. We then examine the current open-source LLM landscape and discuss how leading open models achieve competitive performance through carefully engineered RL workflows.

The core of the talk is a case study on training NVIDIA NeMotron models using NeMo RL, an open-source RL framework. We share concrete results showing how RL improves reasoning, agentic tool use, instruction-following accuracy, and alignment metrics, positioning NeMotron among the strongest open-source LLMs available today. In addition to results, we highlight practical lessons learned and best practices, including reward design, stability and scaling considerations, data curation strategies, and common pitfalls encountered when applying RL at scale. We also discuss infrastructure challenges we have encountered while doing large-scale RL and how they are resolved via NeMo RL. NeMo RL is built to integrate seamlessly with the entire Pytorch and Hugging Face ecosystem, enabling modular reward modeling and tool use, efficient distributed training, and reproducible experimentation on accelerated hardware. This open source toolkit is designed to help teams to try RL on a range of projects, from research prototypes to production-scale model training workflows.

We conclude by discussing future directions for RL in open-source LLM development, including more efficient optimization techniques, richer feedback signals, and emerging trends that will shape how enterprises build, customize, and deploy high-quality open models.

sessions.session_title: Beyond Static Benchmarks: How to Test AI That Thinks, Acts, and Adapts
sessions.session_abstract:
Too often, AI systems stumble not because they lack capability, but because the data and tests used to judge them are shallow, static, and disconnected from reality. Agents are now capable of multi-step reasoning, external tool use, and adaptive decision-making – yet they are still evaluated with single-shot tests that capture none of this complexity. This widening gap between what systems do and what evaluations measure has become a major barrier to deploying reliable, agent-based AI in practice. Gartner research underscores the magnitude of this gap, forecasting that through 2026, organizations lacking AI-ready data practices will see over 60% of AI projects abandoned.

Dynamic evaluation offers a path forward. Instead of relying on fixed prompt–response pairs, dynamic evaluation utilizes workflow-aware, parameterized environments that capture the branching and multi-turn structure of real tasks. These environments make it possible to measure long-horizon reasoning, tool use, and behavior under variation, the factors that actually determine whether an agent is reliable.

During this talk, André Balleyguier, Global Head of AI Solutions Engineering at Snorkel AI, will highlight BeTaL, a recent research effort that demonstrates how LLMs can aid in constructing and tuning these environments. BeTaL utilizes LLMs to adjust difficulty, generate realistic task variants, and expose failure modes that static tests overlook. Rather than hand-authoring thousands of cases, models can be leveraged to explore the evaluation space more effectively.

Drawing on experience across frontier AI labs and Snorkel’s data-centric ecosystem, Balleyguier will outline why traditional benchmarks fall short and how dynamic evaluation, paired with expert-curated data and RL environments, creates a practical foundation for dependable agentic AI. Attendees will leave with a concrete framework for building and operating dynamic evaluations, as well as a clearer understanding of where current systems fail and why.

sessions.session_title: How AI is Reshaping the Data Career
sessions.session_abstract:
There are a ton of opinions about how AI is changing data careers. But most people are so deep in their own work and systems that they can only see what's happening on their team. I have a bird's-eye view on this topic.

Over the past several months, I've had direct one-on-one conversations with dozens of data analysts, scientists, and engineers. Our conversations all start with "how are you using AI in your day-to-day work?" and with each data professional, I go deep into:
- What's working?
- What's not working?
- How is the role changing?
- How is hiring changing?
- What tools are you using?
- How have you upskilled in these tools?

While the level of AI adoption varies significantly across industries, companies, and individuals, I've noticed some clear trends in how the data role is evolving:
- Data professionals are shifting from specialization to generalization, with the most senior folks becoming end-to-end data owners.
- Many data teams are converging around the same tools (like Claude Code), though a lot of it depends on what their company gives them access to.
- There's a growing gap in upskilling that not enough leaders are talking about.
- Conversations around AI safety and guardrails are slowly becoming more important.

Beyond sharing these trends, I'll give the audience a playbook they can use right away: how to start integrating AI into daily data work, what to prioritize learning first, and how to position themselves as a leader and innovator on their team.

This talk is for every data professional who knows AI is changing the industry, but isn't sure what move to make next.
sessions.track: AI and the Future of Work

sessions.session_title: Agents for the Inner Loop and Outer Loop
sessions.session_abstract: Most developers are now used to using agents as part of the inner loop of development. They run a CLI locally on their laptop, making a few changes at a time, and iterating until they’re satisfied with the code.But a growing number of developers are starting to use agents in the outer loop—to do code review, to fix CI/CD, even executing entire changes without ever opening their IDE.We’ll discuss when it’s appropriate to bring agents into the outer loop of development, and how—for certain tasks—they can deliver a huge productivity boost.\\

sessions.session_title: Book Author : Effective Pandas : Patterns for Data Manipulation
Governing Agentic AI: A Blueprint for Safe & Compliant Healthcare Agents in Production
sessions.session_abstract:
Deploying Generative and Agentic AI in healthcare requires navigating a minefield of safety risks and regulatory mandates (NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, HIPAA, ISO 42001). While chat-based pilots are common, moving autonomous agents - systems that plan and act on patient data - into production demands a rigorous new governance architecture. This session provides a practical roadmap for engineering teams to build safety directly into their agentic pipelines, moving beyond manual spot-checks to continuous, automated assurance.

We will deconstruct a production-proven architecture used by real-world digital health companies to govern agentic workflows. Attendees will learn how to:

    Automate Compliance: Map complex regulations into executable policies that gate deployments and generate audit trails automatically.

    Continuous Testing (The "Guardian Agent" Pattern): Integrate open-source evaluation libraries into CI/CD to block unsafe agents before they release.

    Live Red Teaming & Monitoring: Implement 360° production oversight to detect bias, drift, and safety violations in real-time.

This technical deep dive will showcase open-source tools and design patterns that allow data scientists to innovate rapidly while ensuring their agents remain safe, legal, and effective in high-stakes clinical environments.

sessions.session_title: What If the Database Could Reason, Not Just Retrieve? — Reasoning as a New Database Category
sessions.session_abstract:
Current database systems, including vector databases and knowledge graphs, excel at similarity search and relationship traversal but fundamentally cannot perform cross-domain analogical reasoning or causal chain discovery. This creates a costly blind spot: drug repurposing takes 10-17 years, fraud patterns go undetected across markets, and breakthrough mechanisms like CRISPR take decades to transfer between domains.

This session presents ReasonDB, a semantic reasoning database architecture that treats embeddings as primary data and implements reasoning as first-class database operations. Through a working prototype, this award-winning research demonstrates why vector-native storage with probabilistic semantics enables discovery queries impossible with current systems.

Key experimental results demonstrate two breakthrough capabilities: (1) analogical reasoning achieving 7x performance improvement over similarity search (structural alignment 0.207 vs 0.030), and (2) causal chain discovery succeeding where similarity fundamentally fails, tracing mechanistic pathways between semantically dissimilar states (0.708 chain strength despite only 0.074 cosine similarity).

Attendees will learn why similarity is not reasoning, how probabilistic entity representations enable cross-domain discovery, and what architectural primitives are required to build databases that reason rather than just retrieve. The session covers the theoretical foundations, experimental validation, and potential applications in pharmaceutical research, financial fraud detection, and scientific literature analysis.

This research won Best Presentation among 60 PhD-level papers, demonstrating that semantic reasoning requires fundamentally different database primitives, namely, transforming databases from passive storage into active discovery partners.

sessions.session_title: Using Model Context Protocol with Python: A Getting Started Guide for Data Scientists
sessions.session_abstract:
Model Context Procol (MCP) is a growing standard for providing data and functionality to AI agents. Data scientists and software developers should learn MCP from the provider (server) and user (client) side.
Ryan Day will give an intro to the protocol, and explain how it compares to other options for providing context and agency to LLM applications such as CLIs, raw APIs, and Agent Skil.
With the foundation laid, he will share hands-on demonstrations of:
Using MCP servers with VS Code
Creating an MCP server using FastMCP
Deploying a server on the web using Prefect Horizon

sessions.session_title: Building Evaluation Systems for AI Coding Agents at Scale
sessions.session_abstract:
As AI coding agents move from research prototypes to production tools, the systems we build to evaluate their behavior become as critical as the models themselves. This session draws on real-world experience building behavioral feedback pipelines and evaluation frameworks for Claude Code at Anthropic.

We'll cover how to design evaluation systems that go beyond simple correctness metrics to capture nuanced agent behaviors — instruction-following fidelity, response calibration, and failure modes like overengineering or task avoidance. You'll learn how to structure multi-dimensional rubrics that are both human-interpretable and machine-gradable, and how to build infrastructure that scales across engineering and research teams.

By the end of this session, practitioners will know how to design behavioral evaluation frameworks for coding agents, identify metrics that surface meaningful model differences, and transform qualitative human feedback into structured training signals. Aimed at ML engineers, AI researchers, and teams building or integrating LLM-based developer tools.

sessions.session_title: Agentic AI for Autonomous Root-Cause Analysis in Large-Scale Enterprise Systems
sessions.session_abstract:
Modern enterprise systems generate a massive volume of logs, configurations, telemetry, and distributed traces-yet troubleshooting still depends heavily on manual investigation by senior engineers. This session presents a practical, light-technical exploration of how Agentic AI can automate root-cause analysis (RCA) across complex enterprise environments by combining LLMs, workflow orchestration, and multimodal telemetry reasoning.

We will break down an architectural pattern for building autonomous diagnostic agents that can:
• continuously ingest and interpret logs, events, and signals
• form hypotheses about potential causes
• evaluate those hypotheses through structured reasoning
• synthesize findings into confidence-ranked RCA summaries
• guide operators with actionable, step-by-step recommendations

Rather than focusing on code, this talk emphasizes design patterns, system flow, data pathways, and failure modes. Attendees will learn how to structure telemetry, how to apply retrieval techniques for RCA, how to design guardrails for deterministic reasoning, and how to orchestrate multi-agent workflows that emulate the logic of senior incident responders.

Real-world lessons from large-scale operational deployments will be covered, such as hallucination control, signal prioritization, observability integration, error handling, and how to evaluate the accuracy of AI-driven RCA. The goal is to provide a clear, applied blueprint that technical leaders and engineers can adopt-without requiring deep ML engineering or heavy model training.

By the end of the session, practitioners will understand when and where to apply Agentic AI for RCA, how to structure operational data for maximum clarity, and how to design a scalable, explainable, and trustworthy AI-driven troubleshooting workflow for enterprise systems.

sessions.session_title: How to Actually Scale Agentic AI in 2026: a Practitioner's View
sessions.session_abstract:
Agentic AI is moving fast... but most organisations are still stuck on a handful of internal experiments. 
This talk draws on 10+ years of deploying AI/ML across enterprises to focus on what actually works: how to prioritise agentic use cases, design multi-step agent architectures, connect agents to enterprise systems through tool integration, and build the evaluation and observability layers needed to trust what your agents are doing at scale. It also covers the adoption patterns, human-in-the-loop design, and governance frameworks that separate organisations running one agent from those running hundreds.

sessions.session_title: Generative AI & Machine Learning methods for Quantitative Finance
sessions.session_abstract:
Recent advances in modeling of financial markets have focused on understanding deep statistical dependencies among a large number of financial assets and their characteristics. We model joint dynamics of thousands of companies along with hundreds of their financial data fields, e.g. market prices, fundamentals and technical indicators; and when we don’t have sufficient historical data we use generative AI to produce synthetic data. The usage of machine learning to generate synthetic data has grown with the advent large language models that learn the complex multi-variate distribution of the underlying data. 

We show that generative AI has a broad range of applications in finance, including generating realistic financial time-series, volatility and correlation estimation and portfolio optimization. We will demonstrate applications in Data Imputation and Now-casting particularly for deducing geographical, climate and ESG exposures of companies that fail to report on these metrics. We also show applications of generative modeling for general asset and derivatives pricing/hedging use cases, with a special treatment on pricing illiquid instruments which don’t have much market data. Generative models are especially suited for estimating point values as well as uncertainty giving us a measure of confidence in estimation.

sessions.session_title: How to Do Agentic Data Science
sessions.session_abstract:
Agentic coding tools are transforming how we write software, but most data science teams still treat AI as a fancy autocomplete inside a notebook. In this session, we explore what happens when you go further and treat coding agents as collaborators in end‑to‑end data science work: designing experiments, running models, inspecting logs, generating plots, and writing reports, all while keeping humans firmly in the driver’s seat.
​
Drawing on real experiments from my work and side projects, I will share ten concrete practices for “agentic data science,” including how to set prescriptive goals for agents, organize your file system so agents can find and write experiments, encode logging instructions in AGENTS.md so agents can introspect runs, and have agents maintain append‑only journals and reports you can quickly review. We will look at how agents can propose and implement fixes based on logs, generate diagnostic plots that still demand human judgment, and build minimal pipelines first before scaling up full training runs.
​
Attendees will learn how to design agent skills and ways of working where humans lead the inquiry and agents execute at scale, how to avoid the common failure mode of “boatloads of output with no insight,” and how to build taste for good modeling while using AI tools. By the end, you will leave with a concrete mental model and a set of patterns you can bring back to your own projects to make AI a reliable jazz partner in data science rather than an unreliable black box.

sessions.session_title: Executive Round Table : Bridging AI Leadership & Innovation
The Ontology Layer Your Data Pipeline Is Missing
Embodied AI: A Cognitive Shift, Not a Cosmetic Change
sessions.session_abstract:
When AI systems gain voice, presence, or physical representation, they don't just change what the system can do. They change what humans do. People instinctively apply social rules to agents that project presence, hesitating to interrupt an avatar mid-sentence, lowering their voice when speaking to a voice agent, or placing trust in a system simply because it has a face and a voice. To the human brain, embodiment is not a design choice. It is a cognitive switch, one that restructures how users perceive, trust, and behave toward AI in ways that are automatic, measurable, and often irrational.

This keynote explores the intersection of cognitive science and system architecture, covering why embodiment hacks human cognition and the engineering realities required to support it, including the real-world trade-offs among latency, multimodal integration, and model intelligence. The session includes a live technical walkthrough of building real-time voice and avatar agents using open-source tools and APIs, along with an analysis of embodiment's failure modes: uncanny discomfort, overtrust, and ethical risk, and how to catch them before production. Whether you are choosing between text, voice, or a full avatar, you will leave with a new mental model for embodiment and the technical clarity to decide when to use it, when to avoid it, and how to build it right.

sessions.session_title: Decision Boundaries--How They Help us Understand Algorithms from Classical ML to Modern AI
sessions.session_abstract: This talk is about seeing ML/AI through shapes and geometry rather than statistics and code. Whether you're just beginning your ML/AI journey or are a data expert, this talk will help you see predictive algorithms in a new light by emphasizing decision boundaries for classifiers---what they look like for different supervised learning methods and hyperparameters. Then we'll take a peek inside the mind of an AI chatbot and see how these same shapes and ideas help us understand how the chatbot sees and understands us. The talk will be accessible to all attendees and is aimed at anyone open to a more visual understanding of ML/AI.

sessions.session_title: Less Compute, More Impact: How Model Quantization Fuels the Next Wave of Agentic AI
sessions.session_abstract:
The AI industry is shifting from bigger to better. As companies chase efficiency and performance, quantization has emerged as one of the most effective ways to make models smaller, faster, and more affordable—without crippling accuracy. With recent breakthroughs from teams like Deepseek proving that optimization can shake entire markets, developers are rethinking what "efficient AI" really means. The real question isn't whether we can make models smarter... it's whether we can make them smarter per watt, per dollar, and per millisecond.

This session explores the full lifecycle of model quantization and how it powers the rise of Small Language Models (SLMs) and agentic AI systems. We'll cover how quantization works, when it pays off, and how it changes deployment tradeoffs across CPUs, GPUs, and AI accelerators. Attendees will walk away with practical techniques for compressing models, tuning quantization-aware training, and deploying specialized SLMs to leverage them in multi-agent Agentic systems using Agent2Agent protocol. The end goal is to maximize hardware potential while staying responsive without breaking the bank on hardware costs.

sessions.session_title: From Tools to Teammates: Orchestrating AI Agents at Scale
sessions.session_abstract:
We’re moving beyond using AI as a tool and into a world where AI agents operate as teammates.

This talk explores how to design and orchestrate workflows where humans and AI agents collaborate effectively, not just automate tasks. We’ll break down what an “agentic” system actually looks like in practice, from structuring data and defining roles to managing permissions, cost, and control.

The real shift isn’t just in capability, it’s in coordination.

Attendees will walk away with a clear framework for building human–machine systems that are scalable, secure, and aligned with how work is actually getting done.
sessions.company: Human Machine Collaboration Institute | Women In Data™️

sessions.session_title: From RAG to AI Agents
sessions.session_abstract:
As the capabilities of modern LLMs grow, their fields of application also widen. In order to meet the expectations of your customer, a simple RAG application is not sufficient anymore: they expect the chatbots to use tools, to search the web, to self correct, and more. In short, they want an AI Agent.
In this workshop we're going to explore how to take your existing RAG pipeline and, step-by-step, transform it into an agentic system, without throwing away all the work your team has done so far. We will first create a very small RAG application and then upgrade the system, one step at a time, until we have a fully-fledged agent that can take on long running tasks.
This workshop is not tied to any specific framework: we will work from first principles and cover the concepts as well as the implementations, so that you can apply these concepts to your specific infrastructure and tools.

sessions.session_title: Counterfactual Analysis with Bayesian Models: What Drives the Life Expectancy Gap?
sessions.session_abstract:
Across nearly every country in the world, women live longer than men—but the size of this gap varies from about two years in some countries to more than twelve in others. What explains these differences, and how much of the gap can be closed?
In this talk, I present a practical approach to counterfactual analysis using Bayesian regression models. Using publicly available mortality data, we build a model that relates the life expectancy gap between men and women to differences in cause-specific death rates, including homicide, drug overdoses, traffic fatalities, smoking-related disease, and chronic illness.
The model generates posterior simulations that answer “what-if” questions. For example: How much smaller would the U.S. life expectancy gap be if homicide rates matched those in Western Europe?
The talk presents the workflow from assembling global datasets to fitting interpretable Bayesian models with PyMC and generating counterfactual simulations. Attendees will learn how Bayesian models can support explainable modeling and analysis under uncertainty.

sessions.session_title: Beyond the Prompt: Architecting Your Career to Lead in the Agentic Era
sessions.session_abstract:
As autonomous agents and copilots reshape daily work, the most critical skill isn't knowing how to prompt — it's knowing how to architect. This session moves beyond AI hype to explore how technical leaders and practitioners can reclaim their capacity to lead through architectural rigor.

Drawing on nearly three decades in technology — including building large-scale AI systems and data architectures at McKinsey and Walmart Labs, DARPA programs, and federal cybersecurity work at CISA — this session explores the shift from simply using AI tools to designing the decision systems around them. As the author of The AI Decision Advantage, the speaker brings a practitioner's framework for separating what works from what merely demos well.

Attendees will learn how to escape the "Sprint Trap," where AI projects deliver demos but never deliver value, and how to position themselves as the human-in-the-loop architect that every enterprise AI roadmap requires for success.

key takeaways:
Why prompting is a tactic, not a strategy — Understanding the critical difference between using AI and architecting around it, and why that distinction determines who leads and who gets replaced.
How to escape the Sprint Trap — A practical framework for identifying why most enterprise AI projects stall after the demo, and how to design for sustained, compounding value instead.
The shift from data manipulation to decision design — How to reframe your role from someone who works with AI tools to someone who designs the decision systems those tools operate within.
What human-in-the-loop actually means in practice — Moving beyond the buzzword to understand the specific architectural choices that keep humans in a position of leverage and accountability as AI autonomy increases.
How to position yourself as the architect every AI roadmap needs — Concrete steps technical leaders and practitioners can take to elevate their role, their credibility, and their impact in an AI-driven organization.
sessions.track: AI and the Future of Work Track

sessions.session_title: GraphRAG in Healthcare: Enhancing Clinical Reasoning with Knowledge Graphs, GNNs, and Agents
sessions.session_abstract:
Healthcare data is uniquely complex and heterogeneous, spanning clinical ontologies, unstructured medical narratives, scientific publications, and highly regulated patient data that often cannot be centralized. While Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has shown promise in many domains, text-centric RAG pipelines struggle in healthcare settings where temporal reasoning, explainability, data governance, and privacy preservation are critical.

In this session, we introduce a GraphRAG architecture for medical and life sciences applications, combining materialized knowledge graphs, graph neural networks (GNNs), and LLM-based agents. We show how a medical knowledge graph can serve as a unifying layer across ontologies, documents, and reports, while sensitive electronic health record (EHR) data remains virtually integrated rather than copied—supporting compliance, access control, and auditability.

Attendees will learn how an agent-driven GraphRAG workflow plans and orchestrates:
- Graph queries for multi-hop and temporally grounded reasoning
- GNN-based relevance, similarity, and subgraph ranking
- Controlled access to federated and virtualized clinical data sources

Through concrete examples, we demonstrate how this approach enables traceable, explainable, and historically contextualized answers that go beyond what traditional RAG systems can deliver.

By the end of this session, you will understand:
- When GraphRAG is a better fit than text-only RAG in high-trust domains
- How to separate structural medical knowledge from patient-level data
- How to design graph-powered RAG systems that balance integration, interpretability, and governance

This talk is aimed at data scientists, ML engineers, and architects interested in building production-grade AI systems for healthcare and other regulated domains.

sessions.session_title: Beyond the Row: Reanimating "Dead" Legacy Data with Multimodal Agent Swarms
sessions.session_abstract:
The biggest bottleneck in enterprise AI today is not the lack of models, but the prevalence of Dead Data. Most organizations sit on vast repositories of structured legacy data, technical specifications, dry SKU metadata, and siloed inventory logs that are effectively invisible to modern, visual-first consumer experiences. Traditionally, migrating this data to the cloud was the finish line; in the age of Agentic AI, it is only the starting block.

In this technical deep dive, we explore how to architect a Multimodal Enrichment Pipeline that transforms static database rows into dynamic, production-ready digital assets. We move beyond simple "Chat with your data" RAG patterns to a Multi-Agent multimodal architecture. You will learn how to orchestrate specialized agents to autonomously generate brand consistent images, localized marketing copy, and high-fidelity video assets directly from raw technical specs.

The session features a live demonstration of multi agent pipeline: taking a single row of dry product specifications and watching a swarm of agents collaborate in real-time to build a global-ready product campaign.

sessions.session_title: Architecting Secure Data Access for AI Agents using MCP and OAuth
sessions.session_abstract:
The Model Context Protocol (MCP) has emerged as the standard for connecting Large Language Models (LLMs) to external data sources, yet practitioners often struggle with two major hurdles: secure authentication and the complexity of building custom server implementations. This workshop provides a technical deep dive into the MCP Toolbox for Databases, an open-source framework designed to streamline these integrations.

We will focus on the MCP OAuth framework, specifically how it enables secure, identity-aware authentication for AI agents operating within enterprise environments like Google Cloud. Attendees will learn how to bypass the overhead of writing boilerplate code by using modular database integrations that support high-performance data retrieval and operational stability.

sessions.session_title: From Prompts to Systems: What 2 Months of Running OpenClaw Agents Taught Me
sessions.session_abstract:
Over the past three years, we’ve seen three distinct waves of AI adoption: access through chat interfaces, reasoning through advanced models, and most recently, agentic coding through tools like Claude Code & Codex.

But something changes when you move beyond demos and actually run agents continuously in the real world.

In this talk, I’ll share what I learned from running OpenClaw, an open-source personal AI system that automates tasks, manages context, and operates across tools on behalf of its user. Not as a prototype, but as a system that runs daily, makes decisions, and occasionally fails.

Three patterns emerge.

First, the harness matters more than the model. Models are increasingly interchangeable, but the orchestration layer that connects memory, tools, and workflows becomes the real source of reliability and control.

Second, memory and instructions act as force multipliers. Systems that preserve context and evolve instructions over time become more aligned, more efficient, and significantly more capable than stateless interactions.

Third, sustained use reveals what demos hide. Reliability, failure modes, and real-world usefulness only emerge when agents operate over days and weeks, not minutes.

This talk introduces a new mental model: AI is shifting from tools you use to systems that work for you. We’ll explore what this means for developers, product builders, and organizations designing the next generation of AI systems.

sessions.session_title: Small Agents, Big Results: Hands-On Agent Skills with Tools, MCP, and Small Models
sessions.session_abstract:
The default assumption in agent development is that you need the biggest, most expensive model available. That assumption is increasingly wrong. Open source models at 9B-30B parameters are closing the gap on frontier performance, and with the right infrastructure, they can handle complex multi-step workflows at a fraction of the cost with improved accuracy.

In this workshop, you will build working agents from scratch using the Subconscious platform across three core capabilities: browser automation (navigating and taking action on live websites), CLI tool use (executing shell commands within agent loops), and MCP integration (connecting agents to external services via the Model Context Protocol).

Under the hood, we will show how context intelligence allows small models to manage their own context windows during inference, pruning irrelevant information so agents stay sharp across long, multi-step tasks. This is the same approach that posted 70% on WebArena and 68% on OSWorld with dramatically lower compute.

The session targets ML engineers, backend developers, and applied AI practitioners building agent systems for production. You will leave with working code, a GitHub repo, and a practical framework for deciding when small models can replace frontier APIs in your own workflows.

sessions.session_title: The Ralph Wiggum Phenomenon: Evolving Agentic Coding
sessions.session_abstract:
What if the most effective way to use a coding agent isn't a conversation? A new technique called "Ralph Wiggum" challenges us to run the same prompt, to completion, over and over. It sounds almost too simple to work, but the results have been astonishing. Entire codebases have been generated, from simple command-line apps to entire programming language compilers.

Ralph teaches key principles for getting the most out of agentic coding: context management, task decomposition, and automated feedback.

Here's how to write your first Ralph loop in 3 simple steps.

1. Create a PLAN.md with your tasks.

- [ ] Feature A
- [ ] Feature B
- [ ] Feature C
2. - Create a RALPH.md with instructions on how to execute one unit of work.

Study PLAN.md, complete the next task, test, mark done, commit.

The tech stack for this project is...
3. - Run your favorite coding agent in auto-approve (headless) mode with RALPH.md as the prompt, repeatedly. Something like:

agent --headless --task RALPH.md
Next, the weird part: step away from the loop and let it fail. The first version probably won't work, and that's OK. By studying the failures, we learn how to set agents up for success. For instance, if a certain library is misused, we add a note of where to find the documentation. Seeing other issues, we add a style guide and testing strategy. Less and less intervention is needed as we refine the process. Soon we're getting more done and, crucially, it gets done right.

sessions.session_abstract:
