sessions.session_title: Architectural Patterns for Building and Governing Production-Grade Multi-Agent Systems
sessions.session_abstract:
The transition from experimental LLM chatbots to autonomous enterprise agents represents the next frontier of Generative AI. However, moving from "thinking" to "acting" introduces significant risks in reliability and governance. To move beyond brittle proofs-of-concept, architects must shift their focus from prompt engineering to standardized agentic architectural patterns.

In this session, we will explore the fundamental anatomy of an AI agent—Sense, Reason, Plan, and Act—and how to orchestrate these components into a distributed reasoning engine. We will specifically examine the shift toward capability-centric design, looking at how frameworks like OpenClaw are democratizing the creation of sophisticated agents and how Anthropic’s Skills.md initiative is standardizing how agents discover and execute complex tasks.

Key takeaways include:

The Anatomy of Agency: Understanding how LLMs serve as the cognitive core within a broader architecture of memory, tools, and perception.

The OpenClaw Impact: Analyzing how open-source frameworks are providing the scaffolding for multi-agent coordination, reducing the "integration tax" of building custom agentic workflows.

The "Skills" Revolution: A deep dive into Skills.md—explaining how markdown-based capability definitions allow agents to self-document, share, and scale their toolsets across different environments.

Essential Coordination Patterns: A look at Supervisor vs. Swarm architectures and the Agent Router pattern for intent-based task delegation.

Reliability & Compliance: Strategies for implementing Instruction Fidelity Auditing and Self-Correction loops to mitigate hallucinations in high-stakes environments.

Attendees will leave with a practical blueprint for building AI systems that are not only intelligent but also interoperable, portable, and production-ready.

sessions.session_title: The Changing Shape of AI Systems: From Monolithic Training to Continuous Adaptation
sessions.session_abstract: For most of the past decade, AI systems have been built around a single assumption: that a model is something you train once, ship, and then leave alone. That assumption is breaking down. The economics of foundation models have put unprecedented pressure on serving systems and made clear that the interesting research questions no longer end at training time. The next frontier, I'll argue, is continuous adaptation: AI systems that keep learning after deployment, efficiently and reliably, without the cost or fragility of full retraining. This talk is a tour of the open problems in that space rather than a presentation of solutions. I'll sketch how the foundation-model era reshaped the architecture of AI systems, where its assumptions are now starting to fail, and lay out the research questions I find most consequential for what comes next.

sessions.session_title: What Your Agents Don't Know Could Sink Your Product: Designing Human Context as a First-Class Engineering Artifact
sessions.session_abstract:
Every agentic AI system I have ever seen has the same silent failure mode. It is not the model. It is not the infrastructure. It is that the agents have no idea who they are building for.

Every AI agent starts cold.

No user model. No behavioral context. No understanding of who it is building for or why.
I spent two years at NASA leading AI readiness for one of the most complex organizations on earth. Before that, I designed how distributed teams collaborate at Mural and served as an Innovation Catalyst at Intuit, making financial products work for real humans under real stress. In every context (mission-critical aerospace, enterprise collaboration, consumer fintech) I saw the same problem: the richest understanding of who the user was and what they actually needed disappeared somewhere between the research team and the people building the product. By the time code was being written, that knowledge was gone.
Agents inherit this problem. But they make it worse, because agents do not just miss context. They invent it.

This talk introduces Zero Vector Design, an open methodology I founded that treats upstream user research (personas, jobs to be done, behavioral patterns, contextual constraints) not as documentation that sits in a drive somewhere, but as structured, version-controlled artifacts that live in the repository alongside the source code. We call this architecture the Investiture Framework.

The result: agents operating in a Zero Vector project have access to machine-readable human context from the first prompt. They do not reconstruct who the user is from scratch every session. They do not hallucinate personas. They work from a structured, evolvable, team-maintained understanding of the humans the product serves, one that versions with the codebase, survives team transitions, and can be tested like any other engineering artifact.

I will walk through this architecture live using herelabrador.ai, my production implementation of Zero Vector. Labrador is an AI retriever: it fetches the right human context at the right moment in the agent workflow. You will see how Investiture structures the upstream layer, how Labrador retrieves from it, and what measurably changes in agent output quality when human context is present versus absent.

This is not a talk about prompt engineering. It is not about RAG pipelines or vector databases. The point is, those are downstream solutions to an upstream problem. The most critical context in any product development process (knowledge of the human being served) is the one thing nobody has figured out how to get into the machine reliably. Zero Vector is the answer I built. This talk shows you exactly how it works.

Key Takeaways:

- A clear name for the problem: *human context starvation* in agentic workflows, and a framework for understanding why it is an architectural issue that prompt engineering alone cannot solve
- A working mental model of the Zero Vector methodology and the Investiture Framework, showing how upstream design research (personas, JTBD, behavioral patterns) gets structured and versioned as code
- A live demonstration of herelabrador.ai, a production implementation of Zero Vector, showing how structured user context gets retrieved at the right moment in an agent workflow
- A before-and-after comparison of agent output quality when human context is absent versus present as a structured artifact, with concrete metrics on the difference
- Practical starting points for teams who want to implement this pattern: what to structure first, where to store it, and how to make agents consume it

sessions.session_title: Real-Time Event-Time Consistent Analytics Pipelines using Kafka, Flink, and Apache Pinot
sessions.session_abstract:
Real-time decision systems increasingly depend on complex data pipelines that must deliver fresh, reliable insights within seconds. Whether powering trading platforms, fraud detection, operational analytics, or user-facing dashboards, organizations now expect analytical systems to handle massive event streams with both low latency and high correctness. Yet, while technologies such as Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, and Apache Pinot have become foundational components of modern data architectures, the industry still lacks a unified model for ensuring consistency and correctness across the full end-to-end pipeline.

This session introduces a comprehensive framework for achieving event-time-consistent, exactly-once real-time analytics across Kafka→Flink→Pinot pipelines. Drawing from real production challenges encountered at scale—including late-arriving events, reprocessing and replay, ingestion backpressure, and multi-tenant OLAP workloads—the presentation identifies the fundamental sources of metric drift and inconsistency within current streaming + OLAP architectures. Attendees will learn how to apply event-time watermarks beyond the streaming layer and into the OLAP ingestion process, enabling predictable completeness windows and preventing partial or inconsistent aggregates from surfacing in dashboards.

The session also presents an ingestion protocol that combines deterministic segment assignment, idempotent upserts, and Flink checkpoint alignment to ensure replay-safe correctness. The result is a system that reduces aggregate divergence, smooths ingestion latency, and improves reliability under real-world workloads.

Participants will walk away with actionable architectural patterns, correctness models, and practical implementation strategies that can be deployed in any organization building real-time analytical systems. This talk bridges the gap between streaming theory and OLAP practice, offering a blueprint for the next generation of trustworthy, high-fidelity real-time analytics.

sessions.session_title: Build, Test, and Debug dbt Faster with Claude
sessions.session_abstract:
AI can write dbt-looking SQL quickly, but that is not the hard part. The harder part is getting output that fits a real project: models that follow conventions, respect lineage, include useful tests, and do not create more debugging work later. In practice, that is where generic prompting usually starts to break down.

In this workshop, we will use a small dbt project to explore a more practical way to use Claude in day-to-day analytics engineering work. The session will focus on three common tasks that most dbt practitioners deal with regularly: building or refining models, adding tests and documentation, and debugging failures. Along the way, we will look at why project context matters so much in dbt, and how dbt MCP and dbt Agent Skills can make AI outputs more useful by grounding them in the structure and patterns of a real project.

By completing this workshop, attendees will understand how to move beyond generic AI prompting and apply a more context-aware workflow to dbt development. They will learn how to use Claude to speed up common dbt tasks while keeping human review in the loop, how to generate tests and documentation more efficiently, and how to troubleshoot dbt issues with better context and faster iteration. The goal is to present AI as a practical tool that can remove friction from the data engineering work when used carefully.

sessions.session_title: Meet the AI Data Scientist: Automating End-to-End Analytics with Sphinx AI
sessions.session_abstract:
Software engineering has seen a massive lift from AI augmentation over the last 3 years, but data teams haven’t experienced the same lift. That’s because the core loop of data science—working with messy data, forming hypotheses, iterating on analyses, and validating results—is fundamentally different from software engineering. As a result, it requires agents that can navigate data with precision, not just write code.

In this session, we’ll introduce Sphinx AI, an AI data scientist built specifically for data teams. Our system significantly outperforms state-of-the-art reasoning models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google on real-world data science tasks.

We’ll demonstrate how Sphinx tackles nuanced problems across domains—from quantitative research to spend allocation—showing how Sphinx can operate on complex enterprise data, freeing data teams to focus on the ideas and domain expertise.

sessions.session_title: Building Responsible AI Agents with Open Source
sessions.session_abstract: Agentic AI is the talk of the industry, but what does it actually mean, and how do you build an agent? In this workshop, we’ll walk you through spinning up your own simulated support agent for a physician's inbox, using easy-to-use OSS tools like LangGraph and Granite Guardian. In the process, you’ll get a basic understanding of agent architectures, understand how to protect critical data being served to an LLM, and get an introduction to responsible AI agent behavior. You’ll walk out with a first agent that can run on your laptop using open source AI technologies, and an understanding of what it looks like to build AI agents that behave responsibly. Designed for developers who are new to AI agents.

sessions.session_title: Nonlinear Dynamics as the Next Substrate for Intelligence
sessions.session_abstract:
The human brain operates on roughly 20 watts while supporting perception, learning, adaptation, and control. By contrast, today’s data-center AI accelerators operate in the hundreds-of-watts to kilowatt range per device. Complete AI systems compound this cost across many accelerators, networking, and cooling. This contrast reveals a central opportunity: intelligence can, in principle, be realized in physical regimes far more energy-efficient than those used by conventional AI infrastructure.

A key differentiator from traditional systems is that biology eschews the digital computing abstraction of synchronous computation over 0s and 1s. Instead, it executes on a substrate of stochastic, distributed, and nonlinear dynamics. This presentation investigates how in the search for greater energy-efficiency we can move from executing AI models on standard AI compute systems to executing them on the nonlinear dynamics offered by physical dynamical systems.

sessions.session_title: AI in Open Source BI: Building the Future of Analytics in the Open
sessions.session_abstract:
Apache Superset — the most-starred project in the Apache Software Foundation — recently came together as a community to build a powerful MCP server, opening the door to an agentic future for the world's most popular open-source analytics platform.
In this talk, we'll show you what that looks like in practice: how Superset is leveraging MCP on the frontend with a built-in chatbot and beyond, and how Preset has extended the foundation with additional MCP tooling, a proprietary conversational interface, and its newest offering, Agor — a step into agent orchestration for Preset users and far beyond.

sessions.session_title: Trust is an Engineering Outcome
sessions.session_abstract:
Generative AI has arrived and is making its impact, but for many engineers and engineering teams, trust remains a critical bottleneck impeding use.  Probabilistic models often fall short in high stakes environments.  When an AI agent hallucinates a schema mapping or ignores a privacy policy, the result isn't just a bug, it's a security vulnerability or corrupted state.  

This talk will walk through common failure modes, such as constraint violation and semantic drift, and relate them to common use cases in platform engineering and BI/data migration.  We will then address these with concrete, reproducible engineering practices.  First, we will explore the value of "Bootstrap Components" in platform engineering.  We will demonstrate how pointing AI at well-documented, pre-validated infrastructure patterns significantly improves code generation reliability compared to open-ended prompting, leading to higher productivity as measured through DORA metrics.  Second, we will examine architecture patterns that treat governance as code.  For example, a curated spine, which is declarative and versioned, can be used to ground plausible generated outcomes to reality. These approaches help ensure that privacy policies and operational boundaries are enforced as deterministic gates, preventing unauthorized or harmful code execution. Attendees will leave with a blueprint for transforming trust from a sentiment score into a rigorous, engineered outcome.

sessions.session_title: Lessons from Evaluating Production AI Agents for over a year
sessions.session_abstract: This talk covers methods of evaluating AI Agents, with an example of how the speakers built evaluation frameworks for a user-facing AI Agent system which has been in production for over a year. We share tools and frameworks used (as well as tradeoffs and alternatives), and discuss methods such as LLM-as-Judge, rules-based evaluations, ML metrics used, as well as selection tradeoffs.

sessions.session_title: What Does a Data Professional Do When AI Can Do the Data Work?
sessions.session_abstract:
The tasks that used to define the data profession are getting automated. Not eventually. Now. Writing SQL, checking joins, building charts, formatting decks. An AI system can do all of that today, end to end, with no code required. The question is not whether this happens. The question is who controls it.

This talk is about what comes next. I'll share what I've seen building and deploying an AI analyst system that handles the full analytical workflow, from natural language questions through data exploration, analysis, visualization, and narrative. I'll walk through what it gets right, what it gets wrong, and why the mistakes are actually the most valuable part.

Then I'll make the case for where the data profession is headed. In the near term, companies will hire more data scientists, not fewer, because someone has to validate what these systems produce. In the medium term, analysts stop being query writers and become solvers who own problems end to end. In the long term, the distinction between data scientist, analyst, and engineer starts to blur. Everyone becomes a builder.

The people who lean into this shift in the next six months will have a massive head start. The ones who wait for their company to figure it out will be following someone else's playbook. This talk is for the people who'd rather write their own.
sessions.track: AI and the Future of Work Track

sessions.session_title: Solo to Production: End-to-End Ownership with AI
sessions.session_abstract:
Shipping production code used to mean a full team: someone to architect, someone to implement, someone to review, someone to deploy. This workshop walks through how one developer can own that entire process using a coordinated team of AI agents.

We'll go end-to-end on a live example, from architecture and implementation to code review and deployment, using a full suite of agentic tools. You'll see how the different agent modes work together in practice, how agentic orchestration breaks down and distributes work across specialized agents, where handoffs happen, and how to stay in control without becoming a bottleneck in your own workflow.

Along the way, we'll cover how to structure tasks for agents, when to intervene versus when to let them run, and how to use agentic tools to keep context intact across the entire build.

If you've been curious about what agentic development actually looks like in a real production workflow, this is the practical version of that answer.

What attendees will learn:
- How to use Plan mode to scope and structure a production-ready project before writing any code
- How to move between specialized agent modes during active development and when each one is the right tool for the job
- How to run parallel agents across multiple workstreams without conflict
- How to use an AI-powered code reviewer within a real pull request workflow
- How to deploy to production directly from your development environment
- How to maintain persistent session context across an entire build from plan to deployment

Open source tools used:
Kilo (agentic engineering platform)
OpenClaw/KiloClaw (cloud-hosted AI agent)
GitHub (version control, pull requests, and code sharing)

sessions.session_title: Book Author: AI Agents with MCP
How Data Agents Are Killing the Weekly Report
sessions.session_abstract: Data agents are ending the most tedious parts of data work. They watch metrics continuously, surface what matters, and handle the grunt work themselves. Drawing from Squadbase’s early customers, this talk shows what actually changes when data agents take over — why non-engineers suddenly drive data work, what data teams should do instead, and how to prepare your stack for an agent-first world.

sessions.session_title: Domain-Limited General Intelligence: Before Things Go Too Far
sessions.session_abstract:
Artificial intelligence is accelerating faster than our safety practices, and the field is edging toward forms of generality that introduce unnecessary risk. This talk introduces Domain-Limited General Intelligence, a new conceptual tier that sits between narrow AI and the ambitions of AGI and ASI. DLGI offers a path to more capable systems that can generalize within defined boundaries while avoiding the instability that comes from unbounded intelligence.

Attendees will learn what DLGI is, why it is fundamentally safer than the pursuit of broad general intelligence, and how it reframes today’s alignment challenges. The session will explore real examples of emergent behavior, misaligned optimization, and adversarial pressure, then show how DLGI provides a structure to contain these failures before they scale.

This talk gives practitioners, leaders, and researchers a practical mental model for building powerful AI systems with control, predictability, and trust. The goal is simple: advance intelligence without letting things go too far.

sessions.session_title: From Goose to AAIF: Engineering Open, Forkable AI Agents at Scale
sessions.session_abstract:
Over the past year, goose has evolved from a company-hosted experiment into a cornerstone of a broader industry movement. With the public launch of the Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), goose now sits alongside contributions from Anthropic, OpenAI, and a cross-industry coalition of technology leaders. This session offers a technical, behind-the-scenes look at how goose was architected for extensibility, reproducibility, and community-driven contribution, and how those design decisions positioned it as a founding contribution to AAIF’s vendor-neutral, open-governance ecosystem.

Rizel Scarlett, Tech Lead of Open Source Developer Relations at Block, will walk through the engineering patterns that made goose flexible enough to support advanced agent experimentation while remaining fork-friendly for beginners and contributors. She will unpack key design choices, including modular component interfaces, prompt orchestration strategies, and transparent evaluation practices, that enabled goose to serve as a practical, open foundation for building agentic systems.

The session will also explore what changes when an AI agent transitions from an internal project to a foundation-governed standard: interoperability expectations, cross-project alignment, contributor workflows, and the responsibility of stewarding infrastructure that is now bigger than any one company.

Attendees will get insights into building and maintaining open source AI agents, including:
* How to architect open, forkable AI agents using modular components, clean interfaces, and reproducible evaluation patterns.
* Engineering patterns behind goose’s growth; prompt orchestration, tool-use abstractions, and contributor-friendly design that enabled global adoption.
* How goose became a founding project of the Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) and what this means for governance, interoperability, and industry-wide standards.
* How to align agent development with emerging open standards to ensure consistency, security, and cross-framework compatibility.
* How to design contribution pathways so data scientists, ML engineers, and beginners can meaningfully participate in OSS AI projects.
* Why agentic AI is at an inflection point now, and how open, foundation-governed infrastructure is shifting the field from isolated R&D to shared, cross-industry tooling.

If you’re developing AI agents, building internal automation tools, contributing to open-source frameworks, or evaluating how agentic infrastructure fits into your ML stack, this session provides actionable engineering insights grounded in real-world OSS experience.

sessions.session_title: Selling Agents, Not Software: How GTM Motions Must Evolve for the Agentic Era
sessions.session_abstract:
Every go-to-market playbook in software was designed for one assumption: a human on the other end clicking a button. Product-led growth, sales-led growth, community-led growth, all of it built around human discovery, evaluation, and adoption. That assumption is breaking down.

As AI agents become the primary consumers and orchestrators of software tools, browsing, evaluating, and integrating products autonomously the entire GTM stack must be reimagined. This talk introduces a practical framework for the emerging discipline of Agent Engine Optimization (AEO): making your product not just user-friendly, but agent-readable, agent-executable, and agent-trustworthy

Whether you are building an agentic product, selling into enterprises adopting agents, or designing the strategy for your organization's AI roadmap, this session delivers concrete, immediately applicable frameworks.

sessions.session_title: Agentic LLMs in Practice: Tools, Function Calling, and Workflow Design
sessions.session_abstract:
As Large Language Models (LLMs) transition from conversational interfaces to autonomous agents, AI practitioners face significant engineering challenges in designing robust, production-ready systems. "Agentic LLMs in Practice: Tools, Function Calling, and Workflow Design" is a hands-on workshop designed for AI engineers, data scientists, and software developers across all tech-enabled industries who want to build reliable systems capable of executing complex, multi-step tasks.

This interactive session bridges the gap between theoretical agent concepts and real-world implementation. We will demystify how tool-use and agentic workflows operate end-to-end under the hood, exploring how models interpret schemas, invoke external APIs, and process dynamic responses. Participants will explore industry-standard reference architectures for integrating function calling, moving beyond simple linear prompt chains into robust, graph-based state machines.

Crucially, this workshop focuses on the harsh realities of production environments. Attendees will learn to identify and resolve critical production anti-patterns that often cause agentic systems to fail. We will specifically tackle brittle planning fallacies, infinite retry loops, context window exhaustion (memory mismanagement), and black-box debugging (observability).

By the end of this session, attendees will possess the concrete skills and code templates needed to design deterministic workflows around non-deterministic models. Practitioners will leave with actionable strategies to deploy reliable, auditable, and scalable agentic LLM applications that drive immediate automation value within their organization's tech stack.

sessions.session_title: Building FSI Agents with Claude
sessions.session_abstract:
AI agents are quickly becoming a core component of most new products. Understanding how to design, constrain, and ship agents is an invaluable skill for practitioners.
By completing this workshop, you will develop an understanding of general best practices for building agents, including when to use an agent versus a simpler workflow, how to design tools and manage context, and how to package and deploy reusable agent workflows. All examples and the live demo are drawn from financial services, so attendees working in FSI will also leave with patterns they can apply directly.

sessions.session_title: Coffee Break
Why AI Systems Behave Differently in Production: Nondeterminism, GPU Execution Drift, and Hidden Reliability Gaps
sessions.session_abstract:
Modern AI systems increasingly rely on heterogeneous compute platforms — multigenerational GPUs, shared accelerators, framework-specific runtimes, and complex memory hierarchies. While most practitioners assume “deterministic” execution, real-world production workloads often show inconsistent latency, silent performance drift, differing results across environments, and failures that cannot be reproduced reliably. These issues are not caused by bad code — they originate from the hidden nondeterministic behavior inside GPU execution paths, kernel scheduling, operator fusion, SDK-level optimizations, and coherence interactions across CPU–GPU memory domains.

This session demystifies why AI behaves differently in production than in development. Drawing from my experience in full-chip coherency verification of high-performance CPU+GPU systems, this talk walks through how GPU kernels are actually scheduled, what causes micro-latency jitter, why SDK upgrades silently change execution graphs, how hardware memory models affect tensor operations, and why identical inference pipelines can produce different results run-to-run.

Attendees will learn a practical, hardware-informed framework for debugging nondeterministic failure modes in multimodal AI pipelines. We will outline how to identify GPU-level drift, profile memory-coherence delays, benchmark SDK regression behavior, and build monitoring/observability layers that expose deeper hardware signals beyond CPU-style logs.

This session empowers AI/ML practitioners, MLOps engineers, and data scientists with the architectural understanding required to stabilize AI systems at scale.


Why this talk is helpful ?
Most AI reliability issues come from “invisible” GPU behavior that standard debugging tools cannot detect. This talk finally explains the root cause, allowing teams to diagnose problems faster and deploy models with higher stability and predictability.


Key takeaway for the audience :
a) Understand why AI workloads show nondeterministic latency and drift
b) How GPU kernels, memory models, and SDK updates impact performance
c) Concrete steps to profile and debug unreproducible failures
d) How to build GPU-aware observability and reliability workflows
e) A mental model for debugging complex AI inference and training pipelines


How it will help them ?
Attendees will be able to confidently diagnose previously “mysterious” issues in AI production systems, reduce MTTR, prevent silent regressions, and operate GPU-accelerated workloads with more predictability and control.

sessions.session_title: Models are What they Eat: Building Automatic Data Curation at Scale
sessions.session_abstract: The quality of training data is the single biggest driver of model performance, yet manual curation is an unscalable bottleneck and data research remains the single most underinvested area of ML research relative to its impact. At DatologyAI, our mission is to build a fully automated data curation engine to enable anyone to train powerful, cost-effective models on their own data. Based on hundreds of thousands of experiments, our curation already delivers order-of-magnitude gains in data efficiency. In this talk, I will share key lessons from our journey to scale data curation to massive datasets with no humans in the loop. We will explore some of the applied scientific principles we've uncovered for effective data curation, the engineering challenges we've faced in building a petabyte-scale infrastructure, and discuss the biggest remaining opportunities to drive the next order of magnitude improvement in data efficiency.

sessions.session_title: Releasing the power of AI/ML in boosting drug development
sessions.session_abstract: The pharmaceutical industry is increasingly embracing Artificial Intelligence (AI) across the drug development lifecycle. This presentation provides an overview of these AI methods and technologies: classical Machine Learning (ML), AI/ML in Target Trial Emulation (TTE), and generative AI (genAI). Concrete examples of classical ML are presented through predictive models developed to prioritize risk factors critical to progression to severe COVID‑19, demonstrating the importance of model evaluation, interpretability, and external validation. These use cases illustrate how ML can generate clinically meaningful insights while emphasizing the necessity of robust validation strategies for model acceptance. The presentation then introduces TTE as a unifying framework for causal inference using observational data. Here, AI/ML methods are incorporated as powerful tools for capturing high‑dimensional exposure and outcome mechanisms, while acknowledging trade‑offs related to bias, variance, sample size, and computational complexity. Frameworks for evaluating and reporting causal analyses are discussed, along with challenges inherent to unobserved counterfactuals. Finally, the talk highlights the genAI revolution, focusing on document automation, content creation, and data mining. Evaluation of generative models is examined, noting the limitations of traditional metrics and the central role of human judgment. The presentation concludes by emphasizing multidisciplinary collaboration, risk‑based AI governance, and human‑in‑the‑loop design as critical enablers for responsible and impactful AI deployment in the pharmaceutical industry.

sessions.session_title: Building Production-Ready Agentic AI — Why a Control Plane Matters
sessions.session_abstract: Agentic AI promises autonomy, but most organizations remain stuck in pilot mode. Generative models alone can’t deliver the reliability production demands—agents can hallucinate, misread context, and amplify small errors across complex workflows. Without a unifying control plane, scaling agentic AI leads to unpredictable outcomes. Join Dynatrace’s Wayne Segar to learn what it takes to run agentic AI in production. Discover how end-to-end observability, deterministic real-time context, and a centralized control plane enable reliable autonomy—while balancing human oversight, feedback loops, and automation at scale.

sessions.session_title: Panel: From Technology to ROI: AI in Finance
sessions.session_abstract: From algorithmic trading and risk modeling to fraud detection, credit scoring, and operational automation, panelists will explore what it takes to deploy AI responsibly and profitably in highly regulated environments. The discussion will cover model governance, data readiness, compliance, infrastructure, and the gap between proof-of-concept and production scale.
sessions.full_name:
Barbara Widholm | 
Preethi Raghavan | Dr Miquel Noguer i Alonso | Eric Lam | Brian Joseph Hengen
sessions.company:
State Street | 
Fidelity Investments | Artificial Intelligence Finance Institute | Google |  Oracle

sessions.session_title: Distributed Fine-tuning with PyTorch and Kubernetes
sessions.session_abstract:
Writing your own distributed fine-tuning code across multiple GPUs is not trivial. You have to think about data parallelism, communication between nodes, gradient synchronization, and a dozen other moving pieces. It's easy to introduce bugs that silently degrade performance. So why reinvent the wheel?
In this talk, we'll explore two battle-tested approaches: Ray and Kubeflow. Each takes a different path to solve the distributed training problem, and we'll show you both in action.
We'll start with Ray, demonstrating how it simplifies distributed training. You can prototype locally on your laptop, then scale horizontally without rewriting your code. We'll explore its strengths and tradeoffs.
Then we'll shift to Kubeflow, showing a different approach to orchestration and monitoring. One unified interface to manage your entire training pipeline, scaling from a single worker to dozens of nodes trivially. We'll compare and contrast how it tackles the same problem differently.
The concrete example throughout? Fine-tuning Hugging Face's open source SmolLM family into a specialized wilderness and handyman expert. You'll see the full pipeline: data prep, training configuration, scaling, and monitoring—all without hand-rolling distributed PyTorch code.
By the end, you'll understand when to reach for Ray versus Kubeflow, how each framework eliminates the complexity, and how to take what you've learned back to your own projects. Come see it in action.

sessions.session_title: AI Productivity in Business:  Predictions, Developments, and Strategic Considerations
Securing Agentic AI: Building Explainable Guardrails for Production AI Systems
sessions.session_abstract:
As enterprises rapidly deploy generative and agentic AI systems, a new security challenge is emerging: how to govern AI systems that can reason, act, and interact with enterprise data autonomously. Traditional cybersecurity tools were designed to protect networks and applications—not AI interactions. Meanwhile, relying on one AI system to secure another introduces additional opacity and risk.

In this session, we demonstrate a practical approach to AI trust, security, and governance at the interaction layer, where users, prompts, tools, and models meet. Using the Aiceberg platform as a working example, we show how deterministic and explainable AI models can be used to monitor, analyze, and enforce policies across AI applications in real time. Aiceberg uses non-generative models to independently evaluate AI inputs and outputs, detect risks such as prompt injection or data leakage, and provide auditable oversight across AI agents and applications.

Attendees will learn how modern AI architectures introduce new attack surfaces—including prompt manipulation, unsafe tool execution, and ungoverned agent workflows—and how these risks can be mitigated using explainable monitoring, policy enforcement, and observability.

Through architecture diagrams, practical examples, and a live demonstration, this session equips AI builders with the frameworks needed to deploy AI systems that are secure, observable, and regulator-ready from day one.

sessions.session_title: From Prompt to Production: Python-Native App Development in the Age of AI
sessions.session_abstract:
Data scientists and ML engineers are increasingly expected to ship interactive applications, not just notebooks or models. The tooling choices today are frustrating: lightweight Python frameworks deliver quickly but hit a wall the moment you need authentication, custom components, or a production deployment — while full-stack JavaScript ecosystems deliver capability at the cost of leaving the language you work in every day.

This session introduces a third path, powered by the most significant shift in software development of the past two years: AI-assisted app generation, applied to a Python-native framework. We will walk through how AI-generated apps can be built, extended, and deployed entirely in Python, without the platform lock-in or code obfuscation that characterizes most AI app builders on the market today.

Through three concrete examples: a data assistant that chats with a CSV, an interactive analytics dashboard, and a production-grade app with Google OAuth, attendees will see how a single natural-language prompt can scaffold a complete, readable Python application that deploys with one command and is fully owned by the developer. Particular attention will be paid to the generated code itself: what it looks like, how it handles state, and how developers can extend it by hand when the prompt falls short.

Key takeaways:

When Python-native app frameworks beat reaching for Streamlit, Gradio, or a JavaScript stack
How AI-assisted development changes the shape of application construction for the Python community
Architectural patterns for combining AI-generated code with manual editing and version control
Production considerations: authentication, deployment, and long-term maintainability
Whether you are a data scientist ready to ship your first interactive app, an ML engineer building internal tools at scale, or a technical leader evaluating your team's application development stack, this session provides a practical framework and working examples you can take back to your organization.

Background Knowledge: Working familiarity with Python. Prior experience with data app frameworks (Streamlit, Gradio) or web frameworks (Flask, FastAPI) is helpful but not required.

sessions.session_title: Fast, Cheap, and Accurate: LLM Inference in Practice
sessions.session_abstract:
Serving a model is easy. Serving it efficiently is where most teams get stuck.

In this hands-on workshop, you'll build a working inference stack using three open source tools: vLLM to serve models behind an OpenAI-compatible API, LLM Compressor to accurately quantize models into smaller, faster, more memory-efficient formats, and GuideLLM to benchmark the results so you know exactly what you're trading off.

You'll start with a baseline model, compress it, redeploy it, and measure what changed: throughput, tail latency, memory footprint. No deep ML background required. Just real tradeoffs, real numbers, and a workflow you can take back to your own stack.

sessions.session_title: Toward Universal Steering and Monitoring of AI Models
sessions.session_abstract: AI models contain much of human knowledge.  Understanding the representation of this knowledge will lead to improvements in model capabilities and safeguards.  Building on advances in feature learning, we developed an approach for extracting representations of semantic notions or concepts in AI models.  We showed how these representations enabled model steering, through which we exposed vulnerabilities and improved model capabilities.  We demonstrated that concept representations were transferable across languages and enabled multi-concept steering.  Across hundreds of concepts, we found that larger models were more steerable and steering improved model capabilities beyond prompting.  We showed that concept representations were more effective for monitoring misaligned content than using judge models.  Our results illustrate the power of internal representations for advancing AI safety and model capabilities.

sessions.session_title: Panel: AI and the Future of Work
sessions.session_abstract: AI is changing the way we work, but what’s real and what’s just hype? This panel will dive into how AI is truly shaping jobs, skills, and collaboration today. Join experts as they discuss where AI is helping, where it’s falling short, and how people and organizations can prepare for the future of work.

sessions.session_title: Practical Agent Ops: From PoC to Prod with MLflow 3.0
sessions.session_abstract:
Are your agents getting stuck in “PoC Prison”? Are you held up by limited evaluation sets and hardly any feedback? Are your stakeholders waiting around wondering when they’ll get to see the product demo? When you do get feedback, how quickly will it be incorporated? 

The latest major release of MLflow (version 3.0, released in summer 2025) was a major framework overhaul to help address these operational challenges and accelerate the path from proof-of-concept to production for agents and other generative AI applications. With its framework-agnostic APIs, enhanced tracing, dedicated tools for agent evaluation, and flexible deployment options, MLflow 3.0 makes it easier than ever to operationalize complex, multi-step agents. But where should you start? What are the most critical MLflow APIs to learn? What does the right development cycle look like?

At Western Governors University, we’re using MLflow >= 3.0 to accelerate the deployment of our agentic applications—such as AI-assisted enrollment—and drive alignment between data science, engineering, and product teams. By incorporating MLflow earlier in the development pipeline, we’ve seen dramatic improvements: deployment cycles shortened from months to days, evaluation relevance increased tenfold, and cross-team feedback loops tightened to hours instead of weeks.

In this interactive demo, we’ll tackle a real-world use case from start to finish. The session will teach you to: (1) set up an experimentation framework from scratch; (2) build critical evaluations with custom metrics for accuracy, safety, and relevance; (3) perform rapid A/B testing feedback iterations and debugging via MLflow tracing; (4) deploy versioned models to different production environments of choice.

We’ll show you how to apply this framework to simple chat completions as well as more complex agents using popular open-source frameworks. We’ll zero in on the most essential components of MLflow 3.0 and demonstrate how they work together to accelerate the path to production all on your local machine.

Join the workshop to learn how to quickly add powerful Agent Ops to your workflows, boost the quality and quantity of your evaluations (e.g., integrating human-in-the-loop reviews), and ultimately deliver enterprise value with reliable, high-quality agents. Some experience with Python is highly encouraged, as well as prior exposure to machine learning, LLMs, and agents.

sessions.session_title: Leveraging Graphs and Ontology for Better Context Engineering
sessions.session_abstract:
You're probably hearing a lot about "ontology" and "semantics" right about now. This workshop provides a hands-on introduction in Python to learn how to leverage computable semantics, a technology which has an increasingly important role in data governance, and also in optimizing the "context" in context engineering and LLM-based applications.

This tutorial uses information about a long-term Harvard medical research project exploring dementia risks: structured data, content from papers and articles, geospatial data, etc. We'll explore "unbundling" GraphRAG, illustrating how each step works and how you can customize for your use cases. First, by using entity resolution to merge structured data sources, then with a small taxonomy generating a thesaurus to describe a specific domain. This produces a working ontology. We'll leverage the ontology to guide entity extraction from the unstructured content, and build a knowledge graph plus a vector store which are cross-linked. Then using DSPy to synthesize prompts, we'll implement GraphRAG for a question/answer chat bot, showing how the graph data and vector data augment each other, along with helpful graph algorithms and embedding models, to develop context.

Overall, we'll show integrations for several areas of useful open source: RDFlib, NetworkX, spaCy, GLiNER, LanceDB, DSPy, Ollama, PyVis, Placekey

sessions.session_title: Spec-Driven Simulation Modeling: Building and Validating Decision Support Models with Python and LLMs
sessions.session_abstract:
Data scientists regularly build predictive models, but these models are not always the best option for decision support. Problems that entail system dynamics, process flows, capacity constraints, or emergent behaviors are best approached with simulation modeling. Simulations allow us to explore "what-if" scenarios, quantify uncertainty, and test strategies before making changes in production environments.

This hands-on workshop begins with a review of four simulation approaches we encounter in decision support work: Monte Carlo simulation for quantifying uncertainty; discrete event simulation for modeling queues, processes, and resource constraints; system dynamics for understanding feedback loops and policy effects; and agent-based modeling for understanding complex systems and emergent behaviors.

From there, we'll move into the statistical foundations of simulation.  We'll review the distribution families you'll use most often in business modeling: normal, lognormal, exponential, triangular, and Poisson. We’ll use Python with SciPy's stats module to estimate distribution parameters from data and evaluate fit.

The core of the workshop focuses on a spec-driven approach to building models starting with using prompt templates to guide the development of model specs. Using Jupyter notebooks with SimPy, we assume the role of a coffee shop owner trying to decide whether hiring another barista during the morning rush reduces wait times enough to justify the cost. This example shows real-world modeling challenges like non-linear interactions between random arrivals, variable drink prep times, and queue dynamics.

We'll also see how large language models can help throughout the modeling lifecycle. Participants will learn prompting strategies for generating initial code from specifications, reviewing logic, verifying the model matches the spec, validating the model represents reality, generating test scenarios, and documenting assumptions and findings.

Participants will need intermediate Python skills and a laptop with Jupyter notebooks installed. No prior simulation experience required.

sessions.session_title: Building Trustworthy AI Agents at Scale: From Development to Deployment and Governance
sessions.session_abstract:
AI agents are rapidly evolving from experimental prototypes into autonomous systems operating in real-world, high-stakes environments. As their capabilities grow, so does the need for robust safety, evaluation, and governance across the entire lifecycle. In this session, we explore how to build trustworthy AI agents at scale—from defining requirements and testing behaviors during development, to enforcing guardrails and monitoring systems in production.
We’ll walk through an end-to-end approach that connects evaluation with enforcement: translating policies into measurable tests, uncovering failure modes through targeted evaluation, and applying runtime controls to ensure agents operate within defined boundaries. We’ll also examine how observability and governance platforms enable continuous oversight, helping organizations maintain safety, compliance, and control as agents evolve.
By bridging development-time assurance with production-time governance, this session provides a practical blueprint for building AI agents that are not only powerful, but reliable, accountable, and enterprise-ready.

sessions.session_title: APIs to OMG: Transform your API data to fun Grafana dashboards
Build Your Own AI Analyst with Claude Code
sessions.session_abstract:
Most data teams answer less than half the questions their business asks. Not because the questions are hard. Because the mechanics are slow. Writing SQL, checking joins, building charts, formatting decks. Eighty percent of an analyst's time goes to mechanics. Twenty percent goes to thinking. The ratio needs to flip.

This workshop shows you how to build an AI analyst system in Claude Code that handles the mechanics so you can focus on the thinking. No Python required. No SQL required. You configure the system in plain English and teach it how to reason about your domain.

We'll build a working AI analyst from scratch using an open-source framework (github.com/ai-analyst-lab/ai-analyst). You'll see how CLAUDE.md works as an analytical persona, how agents and skills create behavioral guardrails, how a correction flywheel turns every mistake into compounding institutional knowledge, and how a four-layer validation pipeline catches the failures that matter most.

By the end of the session, you'll understand the architecture well enough to replicate it on your own data. You'll also have access to the open-source repo that powers the system we demo, so you can clone it and start building the same day.

This is not a demo of someone else's product. This is a system you build, own, and control. The only person who should automate your job is you.

sessions.session_title: Accelerating Responsible Innovation for Mission Impact
sessions.session_abstract: As artificial intelligence and advanced technologies proliferate across defense and national security, organizations must innovate with both speed and direction. This keynote introduces the Innovation Vectors frameworks, a portfolio-based approach to operationalizing responsible innovation that treats research investments as vectors with both magnitude and direction. Direction derives from rigorous acknowledgment of mission need, while magnitude emerges from the sustained interplay between technical and mission advances. Drawing on Noblis's approach to Noblis Sponsored Research (NSR), the talk demonstrates how this framework reshapes ideation, selection, and portfolio management of internal research investments, replacing ad hoc project selection with coherent alignment to mission priorities. Attendees will hear how Innovation Vectors are leveraged in practice, how they drive greater return on investment, and how they accelerate development of solutions that meaningfully impact mission,  ultimately demonstrating that responsible innovation is not a constraint on ambition, but the discipline that makes ambition worth pursuing.

sessions.session_title: Evaluating Foundation Models for in-silico Target Discovery
sessions.session_abstract:
In-silico perturbation (ISP) offers a scalable alternative to traditional gene perturbation experiments, yet evaluation of foundation models for ISP remains underexplored. We introduce a novel evaluation framework, the single-cell in-silico perturbation framework (scISP), to benchmark ISP models against in-vitro experimental data using biologically meaningful metrics, including cell state separation accuracy, ISP accuracy, and mean reciprocal rank (MRR) for predicting perturbed genes. Complementary functional analyses evaluate model performance across diverse gene categories. Using scISP, we assess two well-known pre-trained foundation models, Geneformer and scGPT, alongside the deep learning model GEARS, highlighting their respective strengths and limitations in simulating cell state transitions and identifying perturbed genes. These analyses reveal intrinsic differences across models, offering opportunities to optimize foundation models for specific biological contexts. Our extensible framework establishes a robust bridge between computational predictions and experimental validation, advancing gene perturbation research and biological discovery.
 
Attendees will learn: (1) how to assess ISP fidelity using biologically meaningful, embedding-based cell state metrics; (2) how to select and tailor single-cell foundation models for specific cell types, datasets, and disease contexts; and (3) how to apply these models for ISP-driven target identification. The session is targeted to data scientists, computational biologists, and translational researchers in biopharma and biotech who are interested in applying single-cell foundation models for in-silico biological discovery.

sessions.session_title: Builders Wanted: Why AI Hiring Keeps Filtering Out the People It's Looking For
sessions.session_abstract:
The demand for AI/ML engineers, quantum software developers, and applied researchers has never been higher. But the hiring processes most companies use were built for a different era — one that assumed the best candidates look a certain way on paper, carry specific credentials, and come from a recognizable pipeline. That assumption is costing companies the Builders they actually need.
This session examines the gap between what AI hiring teams say they want and what their job descriptions actually signal to candidates. Drawing on work with AI/ML job seekers navigating broken hiring processes and founders struggling to find the technical talent they actually need, this talk identifies the specific patterns that filter out high-performing candidates before a single conversation takes place — and shows what it looks like to rewrite a job posting around Key Performance Objectives instead.
We'll look at how language in job descriptions creates invisible barriers — for career changers, self-taught practitioners, and domain experts transitioning into AI roles. We'll examine how subjective trait-based requirements ("driven," "self-motivated," "rockstar") function as exclusionary filters that select for confidence over competence. And we'll show what happens when you replace those requirements with outcome-based definitions: what the person will actually accomplish, in what timeframe, and against what standard.
The second half of the session addresses the candidate side of the same problem. When qualified practitioners don't apply, the most common reason isn't confidence — it's the belief that the job wasn't written for them. We'll walk through a practical framework for reading and rewriting job descriptions, and explore how tools like the O*NET workforce database and structured career coaching can help practitioners navigate a hiring market that often screens them out at the door.
Attendees will leave with concrete before/after examples of job descriptions rewritten around Key Performance Objectives, and a clear understanding of how the language companies use to describe a role determines who shows up — and who doesn't.
sessions.track: AI and the Future of Work Track

sessions.session_title: ODSC AI Early Badge Pick-Up
sessions.session_abstract: Join us for pre-registration and the welcome drinks reception Attendees can pick up their badges, check in early, and enjoy a relaxed, informal gathering while networking with fellow participants.

sessions.session_title: Clustering: The Good, The Bad and The Beautiful
sessions.session_abstract:
Clustering is one of the most widely used and most frequently misapplied techniques in machine learning. While it promises to uncover hidden structure in data, practitioners often encounter unstable results, arbitrary groupings, and clusters that are difficult to interpret or act on. These challenges are especially pronounced with high-dimensional data such as text embeddings and behavioral signals.

This talk presents a practical, end-to-end approach to clustering using modern, open-source tools built for real-world data. We begin by examining why common approaches like k-means often fall short when assumptions about cluster shape, density, and separability do not hold. We then introduce a more robust workflow: using UMAP to reveal structure in high-dimensional data, applying HDBSCAN to identify clusters without forcing arbitrary decisions, and leveraging datamapplot to make results interpretable and actionable.

Through examples, including real reviews and other data types, we show how clustering can surface meaningful themes and patterns that drive product and business decisions. We also highlight common pitfalls, such as over-interpreting clusters, misconfiguring parameters, and ignoring noise, along with practical guidance on how to avoid them.

Attendees will leave with a clear mental model for when clustering works, when it does not, and how to apply a modern workflow that turns messy, high-dimensional data into useful and explainable insights.

sessions.session_title: From Months to Minutes: Vertical AI for Founders and VCs
sessions.session_abstract:
Growth Focus AI is the vertical AI platform for founder-investor performance — bringing "AI Intelligence Inside" to both sides of the venture market. In this live demo, we walk through parallel workflows powered by KNOWMI, our framework-trained AI assistant. On the founder side: investor discovery, stage and sector targeting, thesis extraction, founder-investor fit scoring, and calibrated outreach. On the VC side, the same platform powers the mirror-image workflow: deal sourcing, due diligence, investment decisioning, and portfolio monitoring — one architecture, two role-configured experiences.

Attendees will see processes that typically consume months of manual work compress into minutes, with consistent, outcome-linked scoring. Unlike tools built on static, fragmented public data, Growth Focus AI's edge compounds through dynamic behavioral signals captured across both sides of the workflow — diagnostics, thesis matches, outreach activity, investor engagement, and funding outcomes — creating a closed-loop data moat that sharpens with every interaction. Relevant for AI practitioners, data scientists, and investors interested in vertical AI, dual-sided agentic platforms, and behavioral-signal moats — delivering lightning-speed intelligence that founders and VCs capture by automating workflows and decisions where speed, consistency and results matter most.

sessions.session_title: AI Evaluation from First Principles: You Can't Manage What You Can't Measure
sessions.session_abstract: Many organizations struggle with improving GenAI quality because they don't know how to measure it effectively. This research session covers the principles of GenAI evaluation, offers a framework for measuring what truly matters, and demonstrates implementation using Databricks.Key Takeaways:-Practical approaches for establishing reliable metrics for subjective evaluations-Techniques for calibrating LLM judges to enable cost-effective, scalable assessment-Actionable frameworks for evaluation systems that evolve with your AI capabilitiesWhether you're developing models, implementing AI solutions, or leading technical teams, this session will equip you to define meaningful quality metrics for your specific use cases and build evaluation systems that expose what's working and what isn't, transforming AI guesswork into measurable success.

sessions.session_title: Decentralized Embodied Intelligence: How Physical AI Systems Achieve Multi- Agent Autonomy Through Game Theory
sessions.session_abstract:
In real-world robotics, autonomy is shaped by environments that are unpredictable, sensor inputs that are often incomplete, and communication channels that are limited or unreliable. Under these conditions, embodied AI becomes the intelligence layer that allows physical systems to adapt, reason, and make decisions that matter. This session explores how these principles extend to multi-agent autonomy for drones and heterogeneous robotic teams operating in domains such as defense, public safety, and environmental monitoring.
Drawing on principles from game theory and biomimicry, Denis Garagić, CTO of Palladyne AI, will describe how distributing intelligence across agents instead of relying on continuous, high-bandwidth communication allows physical systems to achieve coordinated behavior that is both scalable and resilient. He will examine how embodied AI supports decentralized decision-making informed by multimodal perception, local reasoning, and intent estimation. Each agent evaluates its own observations, interprets the behavior of collaborators or potential adversaries, and adjusts local policies based on real-time conditions. This approach reduces communication load and preserves energy while enabling persistent coverage even when individual vehicles lose sensing capability.

The session will explain how game-theoretic reward structures guide autonomous handoff, target reacquisition, and coverage optimization. These mechanisms support missions ranging from distributed surveillance in defense scenarios to coordinated response in public safety incidents and broad-area assessment in environmental monitoring.

Garagić will also highlight how biological swarms such as bees and ants inspire the coordination strategies used in drone swarms. In nature, agents rely on local sensing and minimal information exchange, and Garagić will show how applying these principles allows drones to maintain situational awareness, reassign tasks, and preserve coverage without centralized control. This biomimicry-based approach is especially effective in defense and public safety scenarios where conditions change rapidly, and communication bandwidth is limited.

Attendees will gain a technically grounded understanding of how embodied AI enables multi-agent autonomy and why distributing intelligence across agents is essential for the next generation of Physical AI systems.

sessions.session_title: Beyond the Abstraction Layer When AI Optimizes AI
sessions.session_abstract: Most ML teams know the pain: training runs that should take hours stretch into days, model conversions that silently fail with no clear error, and optimization work that pulls senior engineers away from building. The default answer is manual profiling and trial-and-error — time-consuming, hard to reproduce, and impossible to scale. In this session, we present a different approach: using AI to automate the performance engineering loop itself. Drawing on 20+ years of hardware-level optimization experience across automotive, robotics, and HPC, Fixstars has built agentic systems that profile, identify bottlenecks, and apply fixes autonomously, achieving up to 2.8× training speedup and 80× productivity improvement compared to manual optimization. Attendees will see live demos on real workloads, and walk away with a concrete framework for combining LLMs, deterministic profiling, and agentic workflows to close the gap between "it works" and "it runs fast." Applicable to anyone running training or inference on GPU infrastructure, cloud, on-premise, or hybrid.

sessions.session_title: Beyond the Hype: How Enterprises and Teams Transform with AI
sessions.session_abstract:
Join Further for a candid roundtable with enterprise leaders, practitioners, and AI strategists on navigating the gap between AI ambition and operational reality. In this interactive session, we will explore three essential foundations to effective AI: governance and oversight, ops and execution, and solution design for business value.

This session will dig into the work of transformation and the questions that don’t have easy answers: How do you build AI capacity without disrupting the core business? How do you govern what matters without slowing down? What separates the organizations driving real impact from those stuck in perpetual pilot mode?

Participants will examine common pitfalls, learn what often breaks down in real-world implementation, and discover proven strategies for building governance programs that enable AI success rather than slow it. With practical exercises and actionable takeaways, attendees will leave with ready-to-use frameworks to organize governance efforts, design responsible AI solutions, and align their organizations for the future.

sessions.session_title: End of Day Happy Hour
sessions.session_abstract: Wrap up your day with our End of Day Happy Hour! Join us for a relaxed, casual gathering to unwind, enjoy drinks, and connect with fellow attendees. Thank you for joining us , let's toast to a successful day of learning and networking!

sessions.session_title: Train Your Own Small Language Model: A Hands-On Workshop in Model Design, Distillation, and Deployment
sessions.session_abstract:
Small Language Models (SLMs) are reshaping how engineers think about deployment tradeoffs, such as balancing performance, speed, and cost. In this hands-on workshop, we'll build a complete SLM pipeline from the ground up using open-source tools. You'll learn how to prepare your dataset, select and align tokenizers, configure architectures, and apply knowledge distillation to transfer insights from larger teacher models. We'll demonstrate how to monitor training runs, fine-tune hyperparameters, and apply quantization techniques to achieve efficient inference on consumer-grade hardware.

This workshop isn't theory... it's the playbook for practitioners who actually want their models to work outside the lab. We'll dissect real-world pitfalls like tokenization mismatches, vanishing gradients, and memory constraints, showing how to debug and stabilize training runs. Attendees will leave with reproducible code, a working example model, and the confidence to train and deploy their own compact LMs for production workloads, experimentation, or homelab environments.

sessions.session_title: Finetuning Open-Source Models for Real-world Voice Applications
sessions.session_abstract: Open-source models are powerful starting points, but they’re often trained on cleanly collected and labeled data. Real-world applications live in specific domains with their own data distributions, edge cases, vocabulary, and user behavior. In practice, the distribution gap between training data and production data is where model performance often goes to die. This workshop will teach you how to bridge this gap through fine-tuning, specifically for voice applications. We’ll start with examining the properties that make audio uniquely expressive, demonstrate where pre-trained models fall short on domain-specific data, and finally, walk through a full fine-tuning workflow that you’ll be able to adapt for your own use cases.

sessions.session_title: Using n8n to Create Agentic Enterprise Workflows
sessions.session_abstract:
You know how AI agents work and have probably built some with the OpenAI API, LangChain, or similar tools. But getting from a working prototype to something deployed inside an enterprise environment — connected to real systems, with proper governance and observability — is where many projects stall.

This session introduces n8n, an open-source AI orchestration platform built for exactly that gap. Join n8n Senior Solutions Engineer Kendall Garey as she live-builds a working agentic workflow, connected to real tools, with deployment options that fit inside enterprise constraints.

You'll leave with a clear picture of where n8n fits in your tech stack, a working template you can adapt immediately, and confidence that what you build today can ship inside a real organization tomorrow.

sessions.session_title: Enterprise AI: From pilot to production
sessions.session_abstract:
It is easy to create an impressive AI demo, but only a small fraction of systems make it into production and deliver sustained business value.

In this talk, I demonstrate how the Sovara platform tackles the technical challenges that cause most AI systems to fail in production. I focus on three recurring failure modes in AI pilots:
(i) real-world data and workflows are messy and rarely AI-ready, making production tasks far harder than benchmarks suggest;
(ii) defining and maintaining task-specific evaluations is difficult, making systematic improvement hard and deployment decisions uncertain; and
(iii) even infrequent errors are unacceptable in many real-world settings, requiring a level of reliability most systems fail to achieve.

I integrate Sovara into an existing agent and show how it surfaces previously unseen edge cases and turns them into targeted evaluations and systematic improvements. By focusing human effort only where it matters most, teams can detect and address edge cases at the scale required for production-grade reliability.

Attendees will leave with practical methods to diagnose failures and systematically improve reliability, moving AI systems from demos to dependable production workflows.

sessions.session_title: Panel: Building the AI-Ready Workforce: Human-AI Collaboration in 2026
sessions.session_abstract:
Join members of the ODSC AI Advisory Board for a focused panel on what it takes to build an AI-ready workforce in 2026.
As AI becomes part of everyday workflows, success depends on more than deploying new tools or models. Organizations need to rethink how people work with AI, what skills teams need to build, and how systems, governance, and human judgment fit together.
Drawing on experience across national security, large-scale enterprise data platforms, and human-AI collaboration, panelists will share practical insights on:
the most overlooked skills teams need to work effectively with AI
how roles and workflows need to evolve as AI becomes embedded in daily work
where governance breaks down when human and AI decision-making intersect
what leaders should do now to build organizations that are ready for reliable, responsible AI adoption
Attendees will leave with concrete perspectives on how to structure teams, workflows, and decision-making so humans and AI can work together in ways that are effective, trustworthy, and sustainable.
sessions.full_name: Usama Fayyad, PhD | Sadie St Lawrence | Sheamus McGovern | Dr. William Streilein

sessions.session_title: Bridging the Talent Gap: What Industry Really Needs from Applied Data Science Graduates
sessions.session_abstract: Organizations continue to invest heavily in AI, yet many still struggle to hire data professionals who can translate technical models into business impact. This talk examines the widening gap between traditional data science education and the practical, applied skills employers now demand—problem framing, stakeholder communication, ethical AI judgment, and the ability to deliver end‑to‑end solutions. Drawing on industry feedback and workforce trend data, we outline the competencies hiring managers consistently identify as missing in early‑career talent. We then showcase how Bay Path University’s Applied Data Science program was intentionally designed to close this gap through real‑world capstones, hands‑on projects with messy datasets, business translation coursework, and integrated training in responsible AI and governance. Attendees will gain a clear view of the evolving expectations for data science roles and a model for preparing graduates who can immediately contribute to organizational decision‑making and AI adoption.

sessions.session_title: Change Is Inevitable. Data Chaos Isn't
sessions.session_abstract:
Change is the only constant in data. New systems show up. Old ones retire. Definitions drift. Teams reorganize. AI use cases appear long before the data is ready. Yet many modern data stacks either rely on fragile models built for a stable past…or avoid data modeling altogether.
This session is about why data modeling is a strategic decision, not just a technical one, and why Data Vault is one of the most practical ways to build a data foundation that can evolve with the business.
At its core, Data Vault is simple - a data lake organized by business concepts. Hubs represent core concepts, links describe how they connect, and satellites hold context and history. That structure allows teams to integrate data, preserve lineage, and adapt to change without constant rebuilds.
I’ll connect Data Vault directly to modern data strategy goals: harmonization across systems, interoperability, governance and auditability, scalability, and analytics and AI enablement. When data is organized around business concepts instead of source systems, it becomes easier to trust, easier to reuse, and easier to extend as the organization grows.
This talk also shows how Data Vault fits naturally on top of the practices strong data teams already follow: agile delivery, CI/CD for data, automation, observability, and a product mindset. You don’t need to reinvent how you work. You need an architecture that supports it.
Using real-world examples, I’ll show how a durable data model reduces time to value, supports advanced analytics and AI, and lets teams spend less energy on rework and more on impact.
Because change isn’t the exception anymore. It’s the requirement. And your data strategy should be built for it.
