Crushed by the AI Elephant - Why Enterprise Teams Seeing Only Parts Kills Progress
sessions.session_abstract:
Enterprise AI does not fail merely because of technical complexity, infrastructure limitations, or model constraints. It fails because no one is seeing the elephant for what it actually is.

Business leaders see operations and feel responsible for keeping them running.
Data scientists see data, features, and accuracy metrics.
Data engineers see massive data pipelines, metadata sprawl, quality issues, and hard constraints.
Risk and legal teams see exposure and a rapidly expanding risk ecosystem.
Executives see opportunity and want their organization to be part of the next wave of change.

Each group is right, because each is only seeing one part of the elephant.

The reference comes from the parable of the blind men and the elephant, where each person touches a different part of the animal and confidently believes it is something else. No one is wrong, but no one is seeing the whole system. Enterprise AI has quietly become this exact problem.

AI introduces a fundamentally new paradigm into organizations. It disrupts operations, reshapes user experiences, and inserts probabilistic logic into environments that have long been discrete and deterministic. Adoption becomes harder. Trust becomes fragile. Decisions that once felt binary now involve trade-offs, thresholds, and uncertainty. Most organizations are not designed to navigate this shift.

As a result, translation layers multiply. Teams work in parallel but not together. Governance typically enters at the end of the process, when the most expensive decisions have already been made. This leads to rework, delays, and increasingly constrained systems that deliver far less value than originally intended.

This keynote introduces a different way forward. Instead of treating AI as a sequence of handoffs, it argues for a shared blueprint for AI that gives every stakeholder a place to collaborate early, using a common frame of reference. The goal is not rigid rule enforcement, but dynamic decision-making that makes trade-offs visible before significant technical effort is spent.

We will explore practical examples, including how sensitive or low-quality datasets can be flagged upfront, how connected systems introduce downstream risk, how acceptable use and regulatory concerns can be evaluated earlier, how pre-approved architectural patterns reduce cost and complexity for AI teams, and how business leaders can better understand decision impact, human-in-the-loop requirements, and operational risk thresholds.

By creating shared understanding early, enterprises can dramatically reduce the time spent debating what can be built, what should be built, and what is worth building at all. When teams finally see the whole elephant, AI stops being a source of confusion and becomes a system that actually delivers value.

sessions.session_title: From Data to Decisions: A Hands-On Introduction to Optimal Decision Making with Gurobi
The Expertise Upheaval: How AI Promises to Transform the Nature of Work
sessions.session_abstract:
Much of the public discourse on artificial intelligence has swung between extremes—predicting mass job displacement on one hand and near-utopian economic gains on the other. The reality, at least thus far, is both more measured and more complex. AI is not simply destroying or creating jobs wholesale; it is reconfiguring the skills underlying work and reshaping the expertise required to perform it.

In this talk, Matt Sigelman, President of the Burning Glass Institute, examines the workforce implications of this emerging “expertise upheaval.” Drawing on lessons from past technological transitions and empirical evidence from labor market data, Sigelman shows what early signals already reveal about how AI is transforming work — highlighting what skills are rising or declining in demand, which entry-level roles are most exposed, and where new opportunities may emerge. Finally, he will outline the implications for business leaders, offering concrete guidance on the talent strategies organizations will need to build and sustain the workforce of the future.

sessions.session_title: Beyond the Context Window: Anchoring Agentic Reasoning with World-State and Context Graphs
sessions.session_abstract:
We’ve spent a lot of time looking at why AI agents break when they leave the playground and enter the real world. It usually comes down to two big hurdles: multi-modal opacity (the inability to perceive complex data types and integrate their analytics) and episodic amnesia (a lack of persistent memory). We’re seeing context windows get longer every day, but more room doesn't mean more understanding. To build something truly reliable, we need a unified system of record for both how an agent perceives the world and how it makes choices.

In this session, we’ll walk you through a new approach that uses a multifunctional environment to anchor agents in a high-fidelity, multimodal reality and a context graph for clear, auditable tracing of every decision an agent makes. We’ll provide a high-level architecture for building this kind of framework and dive into three key capabilities: a Multimodal World-State, Context Graphs for decision logic, and Manifold Analytics to combine vector and topological analysis. 

By the end of this year, models themselves are going to be commodities. The real competitive edge is going to be your semantic worldview and system of reasoning. We're excited to show you the blueprint for the World-State and Context layers that turn general-purpose AI into specialized, reliable agents we can actually trust.

sessions.session_title: Beyond Vibe Coding: Best-practices for 10x Development with AI
sessions.session_abstract:
AI coding assistants generate impressive boilerplate, but most developers get stuck in "vibe coding"—copy-pasting without understanding, testing, or productionizing. This talk reveals the systematic practices that separate temporary prototypes from production-ready 10x development. Learn battle-tested patterns for prompting, code review, testing, and deployment that transform AI-generated code from fire-and-forget scripts to reliable, maintainable systems.

This is vendor/AI-agnostic (Matt has applied these techniques with all of the major tools/AI's), but will focus on Python/Data side examples.

sessions.session_title: Towards Trustworthy LLMs:  Understanding Limits, Advancing Capabilities, Ensuring Safety
sessions.session_abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable benchmark performance, often surpassing human experts on tasks such as the bar exam, Olympiad-level mathematics, and complex coding challenges. Yet despite these impressive scores, LLMs exhibit what I call jagged intelligence: they can solve extremely difficult problems while failing at seemingly trivial ones, raising fundamental questions about robustness, generalization, and trustworthiness.
In this talk, I will share insights from my research on three tightly connected challenges that arise when deploying LLMs in real-world systems: understanding their fundamental limits, systematically advancing their reasoning capabilities, and building safety frameworks that scale with increasing model power. First, I will present empirical findings on where and why LLM reasoning breaks down, revealing persistent failures in out-of-distribution generalization and algorithmic understanding. Understanding these failure modes is essential for moving beyond brittle pattern matching toward more reliable intelligence.
Second, I will demonstrate practical approaches for improving LLM reasoning using reinforcement learning, with a focus on dense and structured reward signals that provide credit for partial progress rather than only final answers. I will show when RL merely sharpens latent capabilities and when, under the right training setup, it can unlock genuinely new behaviors and more robust generalization.
Finally, I will discuss how increased capability often amplifies safety risks, and why safety must be treated as a first-class design constraint rather than an afterthought. I will present scalable safety systems including WildTeaming for automated adversarial testing and WildGuard for content moderation, illustrating how red-teaming and safeguards can co-evolve with model capabilities.

sessions.session_title: Why Language Models Hallucinate
sessions.session_abstract:
Large language models sometimes produce statements that sound plausible but are factually wrong—commonly called “hallucinations.” We argue these errors are not mysterious failures of architecture or reasoning, but predictable consequences of standard training objectives and evaluation incentives.
First, we explain why next-token prediction in pretraining tends to favor fluent guessing over abstaining (e.g., “I don’t know”) or clearly signaling uncertainty. A useful way to view this is that a model is implicitly solving an “is this response valid?” classification problem; predictable error patterns follow from classic sources of classification error. In practice, this helps explain why models are reliable on well-represented regularities (grammar, spelling, style) yet prone to fabricate details for rare, arbitrary, or poorly represented facts (e.g., obscure acronyms, one-off biographical details), even assuming perfectly clean data.

Second, we discuss why hallucinations persist after post-training: most mainstream benchmarks still reward accuracy/pass-rate and give no credit for abstention, which incentivizes guessing when uncertain (analogous to standardized tests). We’ll ground this with examples from widely used evaluations (e.g., GPQA, MMLU-style tests, SWE-bench) and an illustrative experiment on SimpleQA (verified) showing how making the grading rule explicit can change the tradeoff.

Finally, we suggest a mitigation path via open rubrics: modifying prompts/evaluations to explicitly state how abstention is graded (equivalently, the penalty for confident errors). The session will focus on intuition, examples, and practical implications rather than detailed proofs.

This is joint work with Ofir Nachum, Santosh Vempala, and Edwin Zhang.

sessions.session_title: Conveying Tasks to Computers: How Machine Learning Can Help
sessions.session_abstract: It is immensely empowering to delegate information processing work to machines and have them carry out difficult tasks on our behalf. But programming computers is hard. The traditional approach to this problem is to try to fix people: They should work harder to learn to code. In this talk, I argue that a promising alternative is to meet people partway. Specifically, powerful new approaches to machine learning provide ways to infer intent from disparate signals and could help make it easier for everyone to get computational help with their vexing problems.

sessions.session_title: Building Intelligent Applications: Practical Use Cases for Modern Data and AI
sessions.session_abstract: This session walks through real-world use cases that demonstrate how modern data platforms enable AI-driven applications at scale. Explore scenarios such as building AI-powered apps, implementing vector search and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) on enterprise data, and running analytics across multicloud environments. See how integrated data, AI, and analytics capabilities simplify data integration, intelligent search, and real-time decision-making. Gain practical patterns and technical insights to accelerate your own data and AI initiatives.
